adults.explanation: Acts 7:52 — "The Just One" (ho dikaios) echoes Isaiah 53:11, Jeremiah 23:5-6, and Zechariah 9:9. Peter also used it (Acts 3:14). The title's irony is devastating: those who condemn Stephen for lawlessness have murdered the Righteous One Himself.

adults.question: What is the narrative significance of Saul's introduction at Stephen's death?
adults.option_a: Luke introduces Saul at the moment of maximum hostility to the gospel — "consenting unto his death" and later "made havock of the church" (8:1,3). This sets up the dramatic reversal of Saul's conversion (chapter 9). Stephen's prayer for his killers may be implicitly answered in Saul's salvation — the church's first martyr's prayer produces its greatest missionary
adults.option_b: Saul's presence was coincidental
adults.option_c: Saul tried to stop the stoning
adults.option_d: Luke mentions Saul only for historical accuracy
adults.explanation: Acts 7:58, 8:1 — "Whose name was Saul... And Saul was consenting unto his death." Augustine reportedly said, "If Stephen had not prayed, the church would not have had Paul." Whether or not directly causal, Luke's juxtaposition of Stephen's prayer and Saul's presence is narratively purposeful.

kids.question: Where did Philip go to preach the gospel after the believers scattered?
kids.option_a: To the city of Samaria
kids.option_b: To Rome
kids.option_c: To Egypt
kids.explanation: Acts 8:5 — "Then Philip went down to the city of Samaria, and preached Christ unto them."

kids.question: What was the Ethiopian man reading when Philip met him on the road?
kids.option_a: The book of Isaiah the prophet
kids.option_b: The book of Psalms
kids.option_c: The book of Genesis
kids.explanation: Acts 8:28,30 — "Was returning, and sitting in his chariot read Esaias the prophet... and heard him read the prophet Esaias."

kids.question: What happened after Philip baptised the Ethiopian man?
kids.option_a: The Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip and the Ethiopian went on his way rejoicing
kids.option_b: They travelled together to Jerusalem
kids.option_c: Philip stayed in Ethiopia forever
kids.option_d: The Ethiopian became sad and went home
kids.explanation: Acts 8:39 — "The Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip, that the eunuch saw him no more: and he went on his way rejoicing."

teens.question: What caused the believers to scatter from Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: A great persecution arose against the church after Stephen's death, and Saul was ravaging the church, entering houses and dragging men and women to prison
teens.option_b: A famine struck Jerusalem
teens.option_c: An earthquake destroyed their homes
teens.option_d: The Romans expelled all Jews from the city
teens.explanation: Acts 8:1,3 — "And at that time there was a great persecution against the church which was at Jerusalem... As for Saul, he made havock of the church, entering into every house, and haling men and women committed them to prison."

teens.question: Who was Simon the sorcerer, and what did he want from the apostles?
teens.option_a: A magician in Samaria who believed and was baptised, but then offered money to buy the power to give the Holy Spirit — Peter rebuked him sharply
teens.option_b: A Roman official who wanted to learn magic tricks
teens.option_c: A Pharisee who wanted to join the apostles
teens.option_d: A merchant who sold spiritual books
teens.explanation: Acts 8:18-20 — "And when Simon saw that through laying on of the apostles' hands the Holy Ghost was given, he offered them money, Saying, Give me also this power... But Peter said unto him, Thy money perish with thee, because thou hast thought that the gift of God may be purchased with money."

teens.question: What passage from Isaiah was the Ethiopian reading?
teens.option_a: Isaiah 53 — "He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened he not his mouth"
teens.option_b: Isaiah 6 about the vision in the temple
teens.option_c: Isaiah 9 about a child being born
teens.option_d: Isaiah 40 about comforting God's people
teens.explanation: Acts 8:32-33 — "The place of the scripture which he read was this, He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened he not his mouth."

teens.question: What question did the Ethiopian ask Philip about the passage in Isaiah?
teens.option_a: I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?
teens.option_b: Can you teach me to read Hebrew?
teens.option_c: Why do the Jews not accept Gentiles?
teens.option_d: Where can I find a copy of this scroll?
teens.explanation: Acts 8:34 — "And the eunuch answered Philip, and said, I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?"

teens.question: What did the Ethiopian say when they came to some water?
teens.option_a: See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptised?
teens.option_b: Let us stop and rest by the water
teens.option_c: I need to wash before I continue my journey
teens.option_d: This water reminds me of the Jordan River
teens.explanation: Acts 8:36 — "And as they went on their way, they came unto a certain water: and the eunuch said, See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized?"

adults.question: How does the persecution in Acts 8:1 paradoxically fulfil Acts 1:8?
adults.option_a: The scattering of believers from Jerusalem into Judaea and Samaria exactly follows the geographic pattern Jesus predicted in Acts 1:8. Persecution becomes the mechanism of mission — what the enemy intended to destroy the church, God used to spread it. The believers "went every where preaching the word" (8:4)
adults.option_b: The persecution stopped the gospel temporarily
adults.option_c: The scattering was unrelated to Acts 1:8
adults.option_d: Believers scattered but stopped preaching
adults.explanation: Acts 8:1,4 — "They were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judaea and Samaria... Therefore they that were scattered abroad went every where preaching the word." The gospel reaches Judaea and Samaria not through a planned mission but through persecution-driven displacement.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the gospel going to Samaria?
adults.option_a: Samaria was a region of mixed Jewish-Gentile heritage, despised by Jews (John 4:9). The gospel's arrival in Samaria breaks a major ethnic-religious barrier and fulfils Jesus' words to the Samaritan woman (John 4:21-24). It represents the middle step between Jewish Jerusalem and the Gentile world — a bridge community
adults.option_b: Samaria was just another Jewish region
adults.option_c: The gospel was not welcome there
adults.option_d: Samaria had no religious significance
adults.explanation: Acts 8:5-8 — Philip preaches in Samaria with great success. Samaritans had their own Pentateuch, temple (Gerizim), and messianic expectations. Their inclusion shows the gospel transcending the Jewish-Samaritan divide (cf. John 4:9,39-42). Acts 1:8's "Samaria" step is being fulfilled.

adults.question: What does the Simon Magus episode (8:9-24) warn about regarding the relationship between faith and genuine conversion?
adults.option_a: Simon "believed" and was baptised yet his heart was "not right in the sight of God" (v.21) — he was still in "the bond of iniquity" (v.23). This warns that profession of faith and even baptism do not guarantee genuine conversion. Simon's desire to purchase spiritual power reveals a heart still shaped by self-interest rather than surrender to Christ
adults.option_b: Simon was genuinely converted
adults.option_c: Baptism automatically guarantees salvation
adults.option_d: Simon's sin was minor and insignificant
adults.explanation: Acts 8:13,20-23 — Simon "believed also: and when he was baptized..." Yet Peter says: "Thy heart is not right in the sight of God... thou art in the gall of bitterness, and in the bond of iniquity." Simon's "belief" was attracted to power, not to Christ. The episode is a cautionary tale about superficial faith.

adults.question: Why did the apostles send Peter and John to Samaria, and what does the delayed gift of the Spirit signify?
adults.option_a: The Samaritans believed and were baptised but had not received the Spirit until Peter and John laid hands on them. This unique delay ensured that the Samaritan believers were visibly connected to the Jerusalem apostles — preventing a separate Samaritan church. The Spirit's coming through apostolic hands unified the church across the Jewish-Samaritan divide
adults.option_b: The Spirit was late due to distance from Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Philip had performed the baptism incorrectly
adults.option_d: Samaritans were inferior believers
adults.explanation: Acts 8:14-17 — "They sent unto them Peter and John... they laid their hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost." This is not normative for all conversion but a unique Samaritan Pentecost — mirroring Acts 2 (Jewish) and anticipating Acts 10 (Gentile). The apostolic connection prevents schism.

adults.question: How does Philip's encounter with the Ethiopian eunuch advance Luke's theme of gospel expansion?
adults.option_a: The Ethiopian is a God-fearer — a Gentile on the margins of Judaism (eunuchs were excluded from the assembly, Deuteronomy 23:1, though Isaiah 56:3-5 promises their inclusion). His conversion represents the gospel reaching Africa and the margins of Jewish acceptability. Philip's Spirit-directed encounter shows God orchestrating individual salvation within the larger mission
adults.option_b: The Ethiopian was already a Christian
adults.option_c: The encounter had no theological significance
adults.option_d: Philip met the Ethiopian by accident
adults.explanation: Acts 8:26-29 — "The angel of the Lord spake unto Philip... the Spirit said unto Philip, Go near, and join thyself to this chariot." Divine direction at every step. The Ethiopian's identity — eunuch, African, God-fearer — fulfils Isaiah 56:3-5: "Neither let the eunuch say, Behold, I am a dry tree."

adults.question: What does Philip's use of Isaiah 53 to preach "Jesus" reveal about early Christological interpretation?
adults.option_a: The early church read Isaiah's Suffering Servant as a direct prophecy of Jesus' atoning death. Philip "began at the same scripture, and preached unto him Jesus" (v.35) — showing that Isaiah 53 was central to apostolic Christology. The passage's language of substitutionary suffering, silent submission, and unjust death mapped perfectly onto the cross
adults.option_b: Isaiah 53 was not used by the early church
adults.option_c: Philip discussed a different topic entirely
adults.option_d: The Suffering Servant refers to Israel, not Jesus
adults.explanation: Acts 8:35 — "Then Philip opened his mouth, and began at the same scripture, and preached unto him Jesus." The Ethiopian was reading Isaiah 53:7-8 — the heart of the Suffering Servant passage. Philip showed how Jesus fulfilled every detail: silent before accusers, unjust trial, atoning death.

adults.question: What is the significance of the Spirit "catching away" Philip (8:39)?
adults.option_a: Philip's supernatural removal echoes Elijah's translation (1 Kings 18:12, 2 Kings 2:16) and demonstrates that the Spirit sovereignly directs the missionary enterprise. The Spirit brought Philip to the Ethiopian and then removed him — human agents serve at the Spirit's initiative and disposal. Mission is Spirit-driven, not humanly managed
adults.option_b: Philip simply walked away quickly
adults.option_c: The "catching away" is a metaphor for death
adults.option_d: Philip was taken to heaven permanently
adults.explanation: Acts 8:39 — "The Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip." The verb "harpazo" (caught/snatched) is the same word used for Paul's rapture to the third heaven (2 Corinthians 12:2). Philip's sudden removal underscores the Spirit's absolute sovereignty over the mission.

adults.question: How does Acts 8 bridge the transition from Jewish to Gentile mission?
adults.option_a: Chapter 8 presents two intermediate steps: Samaritans (half-Jewish/half-Gentile) and an Ethiopian God-fearer (Gentile proselyte). Neither is a full Jew nor a full pagan — they represent a gradual widening. The full Gentile breakthrough comes in chapter 10 with Cornelius. Luke carefully shows the gospel crossing boundaries incrementally, building theological precedent for the Gentile mission
adults.option_b: The Gentile mission began in chapter 8
adults.option_c: There was no gradual transition
adults.option_d: Only Jews were converted in chapter 8
adults.explanation: Acts 8 moves from Jewish believers scattered (v.1-4) to Samaritans (v.5-25) to an Ethiopian eunuch (v.26-40). Each step widens the circle: mixed heritage → Gentile God-fearer. The full breakthrough to uncircumcised Gentiles awaits Peter and Cornelius (chapter 10).

kids.question: What happened to Saul on the road to Damascus?
kids.option_a: A bright light from heaven shone around him and he heard Jesus' voice saying "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?"
kids.option_b: He was attacked by robbers
kids.option_c: A storm blew him off his horse
kids.explanation: Acts 9:3-4 — "And as he journeyed, he came near Damascus: and suddenly there shined round about him a light from heaven: And he fell to the earth, and heard a voice saying unto him, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?"

kids.question: What happened to Saul's eyes after he saw the bright light?
kids.option_a: He was blind for three days
kids.option_b: They turned a different colour
kids.option_c: He could see better than before
kids.explanation: Acts 9:8-9 — "And Saul arose from the earth; and when his eyes were opened, he saw no man: but they led him by the hand, and brought him into Damascus. And he was three days without sight."

kids.question: Who did God send to help Saul get his sight back?
kids.option_a: Ananias, a disciple in Damascus
kids.option_b: Peter, the apostle
kids.option_c: John, the beloved disciple
kids.explanation: Acts 9:10,17 — "And there was a certain disciple at Damascus, named Ananias... And Ananias went his way, and entered into the house; and putting his hands on him said, Brother Saul, the Lord, even Jesus... hath sent me, that thou mightest receive thy sight."

teens.question: Why was Saul going to Damascus?
teens.option_a: He had letters from the high priest to arrest any Christians he found and bring them bound to Jerusalem
teens.option_b: He was going on a holiday
teens.option_c: He was delivering temple supplies
teens.option_d: He was escaping from the Romans
teens.explanation: Acts 9:1-2 — "And Saul, yet breathing out threatenings and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord, went unto the high priest, And desired of him letters to Damascus to the synagogues, that if he found any of this way, whether they were men or women, he might bring them bound unto Jerusalem."

teens.question: What did Jesus call Saul that He wanted him to be?
teens.option_a: A chosen vessel to bear His name before the Gentiles, kings, and the children of Israel
teens.option_b: The new high priest of Israel
teens.option_c: A wealthy merchant for the church
teens.option_d: A military leader for the believers
teens.explanation: Acts 9:15 — "He is a chosen vessel unto me, to bear my name before the Gentiles, and kings, and the children of Israel."

teens.question: What did Saul immediately begin to do after his conversion?
teens.option_a: He preached Christ in the synagogues, that Jesus is the Son of God
teens.option_b: He went home and rested for a year
teens.option_c: He wrote letters to all the churches
teens.option_d: He travelled to Rome to preach
teens.explanation: Acts 9:20 — "And straightway he preached Christ in the synagogues, that he is the Son of God."

teens.question: How did Saul escape from Damascus when the Jews plotted to kill him?
teens.option_a: The disciples let him down through the wall in a basket by night
teens.option_b: He disguised himself as a merchant
teens.option_c: He fought his way through the city gate
teens.option_d: The Roman guards escorted him out safely
teens.explanation: Acts 9:25 — "Then the disciples took him by night, and let him down by the wall in a basket."

teens.question: What miracle did Peter perform in Joppa?
teens.option_a: He raised Tabitha (Dorcas) from the dead after the saints called for him
teens.option_b: He healed a man born blind
teens.option_c: He multiplied loaves of bread
teens.option_d: He calmed a storm at sea
teens.explanation: Acts 9:40-41 — "But Peter put them all forth, and kneeled down, and prayed; and turning him to the body said, Tabitha, arise. And she opened her eyes: and when she saw Peter, she sat up. And he gave her his hand, and lifted her up."

adults.question: What does Jesus' question "why persecutest thou me?" reveal about the relationship between Christ and the church?
adults.option_a: Jesus identifies personally with His persecuted church — to persecute believers is to persecute Christ Himself. This establishes the mystical union between Christ and His body (cf. 1 Corinthians 12:27). Saul was attacking Christians but Jesus says "me" — the Head suffers when the body suffers
adults.option_b: Jesus was speaking metaphorically with no real union
adults.option_c: Saul was literally attacking Jesus
adults.option_d: The "me" refers to God the Father
adults.explanation: Acts 9:4-5 — "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?... I am Jesus whom thou persecutest." The identification of Christ with His church is the foundation of Paul's later "body of Christ" theology (1 Corinthians 12:12-27, Ephesians 5:29-30). What is done to believers is done to their Lord.

adults.question: How does Saul's conversion demonstrate the sovereign, initiative-taking nature of divine grace?
adults.option_a: Saul was not seeking Christ — he was actively persecuting Him. His conversion was wholly God's initiative: the light, the voice, the commission through Ananias were all divine actions. Saul contributed nothing but hostility. This is the paradigmatic case of grace overcoming opposition, demonstrating that conversion is God's work, not human achievement
adults.option_b: Saul was already sympathetic to Christianity
adults.option_c: Saul chose to convert on his own
adults.option_d: Ananias persuaded Saul through arguments
adults.explanation: Acts 9:1-6 — Saul was "breathing out threatenings and slaughter" (v.1) when Christ intercepted him. He was the most unlikely convert. Paul himself later reflects: "By the grace of God I am what I am" (1 Corinthians 15:10). His conversion is Exhibit A of prevenient, sovereign grace.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus telling Ananias that Saul would suffer "great things" for His name?
adults.option_a: Jesus reveals that Saul's calling includes suffering as an essential component, not an accidental obstacle. The persecutor will become the persecuted. This redefines apostleship: bearing Christ's name means bearing His cross. Paul's later catalogue of sufferings (2 Corinthians 11:23-28) fulfils this prediction precisely
adults.option_b: Suffering was optional for Paul
adults.option_c: This prediction was never fulfilled
adults.option_d: Jesus was threatening Saul as punishment
adults.explanation: Acts 9:16 — "For I will shew him how great things he must suffer for my name's sake." The word "must" (dei) indicates divine necessity. Suffering is not incidental to Paul's mission but integral to it (cf. Philippians 3:10, Colossians 1:24).

adults.question: What role does Ananias play in Saul's conversion, and what does this reveal about the body of Christ?
adults.option_a: Ananias is an ordinary disciple — not an apostle — yet God uses him to lay hands on the church's greatest persecutor. This demonstrates that God mediates grace through the community of believers. Even the apostle to the Gentiles needed a simple disciple to welcome him, heal him, and connect him to the church
adults.option_b: Ananias was one of the twelve apostles
adults.option_c: Saul did not need Ananias at all
adults.option_d: Ananias converted Saul through persuasion
adults.explanation: Acts 9:10-18 — Ananias is called simply "a certain disciple" (v.10). He overcomes understandable fear to obey God, addresses Saul as "Brother Saul" (v.17), and mediates healing and the Spirit. God could have done everything directly but chose to use a community member — showing the church is Christ's instrument.

adults.question: How does Barnabas's advocacy for Saul (9:26-27) illustrate the role of mediators in the community of faith?
adults.option_a: When the Jerusalem disciples feared Saul (understandably), Barnabas ("son of encouragement") risked his reputation by vouching for him, bringing him to the apostles and testifying to his conversion. Without Barnabas's bridge-building, Saul might have been permanently excluded. This shows the crucial role of trusted intermediaries in reconciliation
adults.option_b: Barnabas was required to vouch for all newcomers
adults.option_c: The apostles accepted Saul immediately
adults.option_d: Barnabas acted selfishly for personal gain
adults.explanation: Acts 9:26-27 — "When Saul was come to Jerusalem... they were all afraid of him... But Barnabas took him, and brought him to the apostles, and declared unto them how he had seen the Lord." Barnabas bridges distrust with testimony, risking his standing to include the converted persecutor.

adults.question: What is the significance of Peter's miracles at Lydda and Joppa (healing Aeneas, raising Tabitha)?
adults.option_a: These miracles parallel Jesus' healings and Elijah/Elisha's miracles, validating Peter's apostolic authority. They also serve narratively to position Peter in Joppa — on the coast, near Gentile territory — setting the stage for the Cornelius episode in chapter 10. Luke shows that the same power that worked through Jesus now works through His apostles
adults.option_b: They were Peter's only miracles
adults.option_c: The miracles had no narrative purpose
adults.option_d: Peter performed them for personal fame
adults.explanation: Acts 9:34,40 — "Aeneas, Jesus Christ maketh thee whole" (v.34) and "Tabitha, arise" (v.40, cf. Jesus' "Talitha cumi," Mark 5:41). The Tabitha miracle echoes Elisha raising the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:32-35). Peter at Joppa (=Jonah's embarkation port) prepares for the Gentile mission call.

adults.question: Why does Luke record Paul's conversion three times in Acts (chapters 9, 22, and 26)?
adults.option_a: The triple telling underscores its supreme importance in Luke's narrative — Saul's conversion is the pivotal event that redirects the mission from exclusively Jewish to universally Gentile. Each retelling adapts the story to its audience (narrator, Jewish crowd, King Agrippa), revealing new details and emphasising different aspects of the calling
adults.option_b: Luke made a copying error
adults.option_c: Each account contradicts the others
adults.option_d: Only one account is historically accurate
adults.explanation: Acts 9 (third-person narrative), 22 (Paul's defence to Jewish crowd), 26 (Paul before Agrippa). The triple repetition is Luke's literary equivalent of triple emphasis — this event is foundational. Each version adds context appropriate to the audience while maintaining essential consistency.

adults.question: What does Peter staying with Simon the tanner (9:43) subtly foreshadow?
adults.option_a: Tanners were considered ritually unclean by Jews because they handled dead animal skins. Peter lodging with a tanner shows his ritual boundaries already loosening — preparing him for the vision in chapter 10 where God declares all foods clean. Luke subtly shows Peter being moved by the Spirit toward Gentile inclusion before the explicit revelation
adults.option_b: Tanning was a prestigious Jewish profession
adults.option_c: Peter chose the house randomly
adults.option_d: Simon the tanner was a Pharisee
adults.explanation: Acts 9:43 — "He tarried many days in Joppa with one Simon a tanner." Tanners worked with dead animals (ritually defiling in Jewish law). Peter's willingness to stay in a tanner's house signals his readiness for the revolutionary vision of Acts 10. Luke's detail is theologically purposeful.

kids.question: Who was Cornelius?
kids.option_a: A Roman centurion who prayed to God and gave much money to the poor
kids.option_b: A Jewish priest in the temple
kids.option_c: A fisherman from Galilee
kids.explanation: Acts 10:1-2 — "There was a certain man in Caesarea called Cornelius, a centurion of the band called the Italian band, A devout man, and one that feared God with all his house, which gave much alms to the people, and prayed to God alway."

kids.question: What strange vision did Peter see on the rooftop?
kids.option_a: A great sheet coming down from heaven full of all kinds of animals, and God told him to eat
kids.option_b: A ladder reaching up to heaven
kids.option_c: A burning bush
kids.option_d: A star falling from the sky
kids.explanation: Acts 10:11-13 — "And saw heaven opened, and a certain vessel descending unto him, as it had been a great sheet... Wherein were all manner of fourfooted beasts... And there came a voice to him, Rise, Peter; kill, and eat."

kids.question: What happened while Peter was still speaking to Cornelius and his household?
kids.option_a: The Holy Ghost fell on all of them and they began speaking in tongues and praising God
kids.option_b: A storm destroyed the house
kids.option_c: The Roman soldiers arrived
kids.explanation: Acts 10:44-46 — "While Peter yet spake these words, the Holy Ghost fell on all them which heard the word... For they heard them speak with tongues, and magnify God."

teens.question: What did Peter learn from his vision of the sheet with unclean animals?
teens.option_a: God told him "What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common" — meaning he should not call any person unclean or refuse to visit Gentiles
teens.option_b: He should eat more meat in his diet
teens.option_c: All dietary laws were originally wrong
teens.option_d: He should become a farmer instead of a fisherman
teens.explanation: Acts 10:15,28 — "What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common... God hath shewed me that I should not call any man common or unclean."

teens.question: What important truth did Peter declare at Cornelius's house?
teens.option_a: God is no respecter of persons — in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him
teens.option_b: Only Jews can receive salvation
teens.option_c: Roman citizens are superior to all others
teens.option_d: Only people in Jerusalem can be saved
teens.explanation: Acts 10:34-35 — "Of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons: But in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him."

teens.question: How did the Jewish believers who came with Peter react to the Gentiles receiving the Holy Spirit?
teens.option_a: They were astonished that the gift of the Holy Ghost was poured out on the Gentiles also
teens.option_b: They were angry and wanted to leave
teens.option_c: They expected this to happen
teens.option_d: They did not notice anything unusual
teens.explanation: Acts 10:45 — "And they of the circumcision which believed were astonished, as many as came with Peter, because that on the Gentiles also was poured out the gift of the Holy Ghost."

teens.question: What did Peter command after the Holy Spirit fell on the Gentiles?
teens.option_a: He commanded them to be baptised in the name of the Lord
teens.option_b: He told them to go to Jerusalem first
teens.option_c: He said they must be circumcised before baptism
teens.option_d: He asked them to wait for further instructions
teens.explanation: Acts 10:48 — "And he commanded them to be baptized in the name of the Lord."

teens.question: What did the angel tell Cornelius to do?
teens.option_a: Send men to Joppa and call for Simon Peter, who would tell him words by which he and his house would be saved
teens.option_b: Build an altar and offer sacrifices
teens.option_c: Travel to Jerusalem to visit the temple
teens.option_d: Give all his money to the poor immediately
teens.explanation: Acts 10:5-6 and 11:14 — "Send men to Joppa, and call for Simon, whose surname is Peter... Who shall tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved."

adults.question: Why is the Cornelius episode the most significant turning point in Acts?
adults.option_a: It marks the definitive inclusion of Gentiles in the people of God without requiring circumcision or Torah observance. God initiates at every level — sending an angel to Cornelius, giving Peter the vision, directing the Spirit to fall on uncircumcised Gentiles. The episode establishes the theological precedent that Peter cites at the Jerusalem Council (15:7-11)
adults.option_b: It was a minor incident quickly forgotten
adults.option_c: Gentiles were already fully included before this
adults.option_d: Only Cornelius's household was affected
adults.explanation: Acts 10 is the Gentile Pentecost. God acts at every stage: angel to Cornelius (v.3), vision to Peter (v.10-16), Spirit's command (v.19-20), Spirit falling on Gentiles (v.44). Peter later cites this event as decisive proof that God accepted Gentiles (15:7-9). It changes the church forever.

adults.question: How does Peter's rooftop vision function on multiple levels?
adults.option_a: The vision operates literally (clean/unclean foods) and metaphorically (clean/unclean people). Peter initially understands it as about food, but the Spirit's instruction to go with Cornelius's men reveals its deeper meaning: the Jewish purity system that separated Jews from Gentiles has been transcended in Christ. "What God hath cleansed, call not thou common" applies to people, not just food
adults.option_b: The vision was only about dietary laws
adults.option_c: Peter never understood the vision
adults.option_d: The vision was a hallucination from hunger
adults.explanation: Acts 10:14-15,28 — Peter says "Not so, Lord; for I have never eaten any thing that is common or unclean." Three repetitions (v.16) emphasise the point. Peter's own interpretation (v.28): "God hath shewed me that I should not call any man common or unclean." The food vision is a parable about people.

adults.question: What does the Holy Spirit falling on Gentiles before baptism (10:44-48) establish theologically?
adults.option_a: The Spirit's sovereign descent on unbaptised, uncircumcised Gentiles demonstrates that God accepts Gentiles on the basis of faith alone — not through Jewish ritual prerequisites. This reverses the expected order (believe → baptise → receive Spirit) and removes any basis for requiring Gentile conversion to Judaism as a precondition for salvation
adults.option_b: Baptism is unnecessary
adults.option_c: The order was normal and expected
adults.option_d: The Spirit came because of Cornelius's good works
adults.explanation: Acts 10:44-47 — "While Peter yet spake... the Holy Ghost fell on all them which heard the word." Peter's response: "Can any man forbid water, that these should not be baptized, which have received the Holy Ghost as well as we?" God's initiative precedes human ritual — grace is not mediated through ceremony.

adults.question: How does Peter's sermon to Cornelius (10:34-43) represent a gospel summary for Gentile audiences?
adults.option_a: Peter adapts the kerygma for a Gentile audience: he begins with God's impartiality (v.34-35), summarises Jesus' ministry from baptism to crucifixion (v.37-39), proclaims the resurrection with eyewitness testimony (v.40-41), and announces universal judgment and forgiveness through faith in His name (v.42-43). It is the template for Gentile evangelism
adults.option_b: Peter preached the same sermon as at Pentecost
adults.option_c: The sermon had no structure
adults.option_d: Peter only discussed Jewish law
adults.explanation: Acts 10:34-43 — The sermon moves from universal principle (God is no respecter of persons) through historical narrative (Jesus' baptism, ministry, death, resurrection) to universal application (forgiveness through His name to all who believe). It adapts the core kerygma for non-Jewish ears.

adults.question: What does "God is no respecter of persons" (10:34) mean in its original context?
adults.option_a: The Greek prosopolemptes means God does not "receive faces" — He does not judge by external markers of ethnicity, social status, or religious heritage. Peter's realisation overturns a lifetime of Jewish exclusivism. It does not mean all religions are equally valid but that God's salvation is available to all nations on equal terms through Christ
adults.option_b: God treats everyone the same regardless of their response to Him
adults.option_c: All religions are equally true
adults.option_d: Only Peter learned this truth
adults.explanation: Acts 10:34-35 — "God is no respecter of persons: But in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him." This echoes Deuteronomy 10:17 but extends it: access to God is not ethnically restricted. The qualifier — "feareth him and worketh righteousness" — shows response to God still matters.

adults.question: What is the significance of the parallel between the Jewish Pentecost (Acts 2) and the Gentile Pentecost (Acts 10)?
adults.option_a: Peter explicitly connects the two events: "the Holy Ghost fell on them, as on us at the beginning" (11:15). The same Spirit, same manifestation (tongues), same gift — God makes no distinction. This parallel demonstrates that Gentile inclusion is not a secondary category but equal participation in the same Pentecostal reality
adults.option_b: There is no parallel between the two events
adults.option_c: The Gentile experience was inferior
adults.option_d: Only Jerusalem believers received the true Spirit
adults.explanation: Acts 10:47, 11:15,17 — "The Holy Ghost fell on them, as on us at the beginning." "God gave them the like gift as he did unto us." Peter sees Cornelius's experience as a repeat of Pentecost — same God, same Spirit, same gift, no distinction. The parallel is theologically decisive.

adults.question: How does Luke's threefold repetition of the Cornelius story (chapters 10, 11, 15) indicate its importance?
adults.option_a: Like Paul's conversion (3 tellings), the Cornelius event is narrated three times — in chapter 10 (event), chapter 11 (Peter's defence to Jerusalem), and chapter 15 (Peter's argument at the council). Triple repetition in Luke's narrative signals supreme importance. The Gentile inclusion is the second most pivotal event in Acts after Pentecost
adults.option_b: Luke was being repetitive by mistake
adults.option_c: Each account describes a different event
adults.option_d: Only one telling is accurate
adults.explanation: Acts 10 (narrative), 11:1-18 (Peter's retelling to Jerusalem), 15:7-11 (Peter's testimony at the council). Each repetition serves a different function: establishing the event, defending it to sceptics, and using it as precedent for church policy.

adults.question: What does Cornelius's character (devout, prayerful, generous, God-fearing) teach about the relationship between pre-conversion piety and saving faith?
adults.option_a: Cornelius was genuinely pious and his prayers "came up for a memorial before God" (v.4), yet he still needed to hear the gospel and believe in Jesus for salvation (11:14). His piety made him receptive but did not save him — it prepared the soil but only the gospel seed produces salvation. Pre-conversion seeking is real but insufficient without Christ
adults.option_b: Cornelius was already saved before Peter arrived
adults.option_c: His good works earned him salvation
adults.option_d: Pre-conversion piety is irrelevant to God
adults.explanation: Acts 10:2,4 and 11:14 — Cornelius was devout, prayerful, and generous, and God heard his prayers. Yet the angel says Peter will "tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved" — implying he was not yet saved. Piety prepared him for the gospel but did not replace it.

kids.question: What did the Jewish believers in Jerusalem criticise Peter for?
kids.option_a: Going into the home of uncircumcised Gentiles and eating with them
kids.option_b: Not praying enough
kids.option_c: Spending too much money
kids.explanation: Acts 11:2-3 — "And when Peter was come up to Jerusalem, they that were of the circumcision contended with him, Saying, Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them."

kids.question: In what city were believers first called Christians?
kids.option_a: Antioch
kids.option_b: Jerusalem
kids.option_c: Rome
kids.explanation: Acts 11:26 — "And the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch."

kids.question: What did the believers in Antioch do when they heard about a famine?
kids.option_a: They sent relief to the brethren in Judaea, each according to their ability
kids.option_b: They moved to another country
kids.option_c: They stored food only for themselves
kids.explanation: Acts 11:29 — "Then the disciples, every man according to his ability, determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judaea."

teens.question: How did Peter defend his visit to Cornelius?
teens.option_a: He told the whole story from beginning to end — the vision, the Spirit's command, and how the Holy Ghost fell on the Gentiles — and asked "What was I, that I could withstand God?"
teens.option_b: He refused to explain himself
teens.option_c: He apologised and said he would not do it again
teens.option_d: He said Cornelius was really a Jew
teens.explanation: Acts 11:4-17 — Peter rehearsed the matter in order, concluding: "Forasmuch then as God gave them the like gift as he did unto us, who believed on the Lord Jesus Christ; what was I, that I could withstand God?"

teens.question: How did the Jerusalem believers respond after hearing Peter's explanation?
teens.option_a: They held their peace and glorified God, saying "Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life"
teens.option_b: They expelled Peter from the church
teens.option_c: They decided Gentiles needed circumcision first
teens.option_d: They ignored Peter completely
teens.explanation: Acts 11:18 — "When they heard these things, they held their peace, and glorified God, saying, Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life."

teens.question: Who was sent by the Jerusalem church to encourage the believers in Antioch?
teens.option_a: Barnabas, who was glad when he saw the grace of God and exhorted them all to cleave unto the Lord
teens.option_b: Peter, who established a new church there
teens.option_c: James, who laid down the law
teens.option_d: John, who wrote them a letter
teens.explanation: Acts 11:22-23 — "They sent forth Barnabas, that he should go as far as Antioch. Who, when he came, and had seen the grace of God, was glad, and exhorted them all, that with purpose of heart they would cleave unto the Lord."

teens.question: Who did Barnabas go and find to help him in the work at Antioch?
teens.option_a: Saul, whom he brought from Tarsus — and they taught together for a whole year
teens.option_b: Peter, from Jerusalem
teens.option_c: John Mark, from Antioch
teens.option_d: Timothy, from Lystra
teens.explanation: Acts 11:25-26 — "Then departed Barnabas to Tarsus, for to seek Saul: And when he had found him, he brought him unto Antioch. And it came to pass, that a whole year they assembled themselves with the church, and taught much people."

teens.question: What prophet predicted the great famine that happened under Emperor Claudius?
teens.option_a: Agabus, who signified by the Spirit that there would be a great dearth throughout all the world
teens.option_b: Isaiah, who wrote about it centuries earlier
teens.option_c: Elijah, who appeared in a vision
teens.option_d: John the Baptist, who had warned about it
teens.explanation: Acts 11:28 — "And there stood up one of them named Agabus, and signified by the Spirit that there should be great dearth throughout all the world: which came to pass in the days of Claudius Caesar."

adults.question: What does the Jerusalem church's initial reaction to Peter (11:2-3) reveal about the challenge of Gentile inclusion?
adults.option_a: Even after Pentecost, the Jerusalem believers' instinct was to maintain Jewish-Gentile boundaries. Their objection was not about conversion but about table fellowship — eating with uncircumcised Gentiles violated purity norms. This shows that theological breakthroughs (God accepts Gentiles) must also become practical realities (Jews and Gentiles eat together)
adults.option_b: The Jerusalem church was immediately supportive
adults.option_c: Table fellowship was unimportant
adults.option_d: Peter was criticised for the wrong reasons
adults.explanation: Acts 11:2-3 — "They of the circumcision contended with him, Saying, Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them." The issue is table fellowship — the social expression of spiritual inclusion. This same tension erupts again in Galatians 2:11-14. Theology must reshape practice.

adults.question: What is the significance of Antioch becoming the new centre of Gentile Christianity?
adults.option_a: Antioch (third-largest city in the Roman Empire) becomes the base for Gentile mission, shifting the church's centre of gravity from Jerusalem. Its mixed Jewish-Gentile church models the new community that transcends ethnic boundaries. From Antioch, the missionary journeys launch (13:1-3). It represents the church becoming truly universal
adults.option_b: Antioch was unimportant in early Christianity
adults.option_c: Jerusalem remained the sole centre throughout
adults.option_d: Antioch was chosen for its climate
adults.explanation: Acts 11:19-26 — Scattered believers preach to "Grecians" (Hellenistic Gentiles) in Antioch. The church grows, Barnabas and Saul teach for a year, and "the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch." The shift from Jerusalem to Antioch mirrors the shift from Jewish to Gentile mission.

adults.question: What does the name "Christian" (Christianos) tell us about how the early church was perceived?
adults.option_a: The suffix "-ianos" was used for followers of a political leader (like Caesariani, Herodiani). Being called "Christians" meant outsiders saw the disciples as followers/partisans of Christos (Messiah/Christ). The name reflects that the early church's identity was so defined by Christ that pagans coined a political-style label for them
adults.option_b: The disciples named themselves Christians
adults.option_c: The term was a Jewish insult
adults.option_d: It was an official Roman designation
adults.explanation: Acts 11:26 — "The disciples were called Christians first in Antioch." The passive "were called" (chrematisai) may indicate divine designation or public labelling. The "-ianos" suffix (Latin formation) suggests Gentile pagans coined it — identifying the movement by its allegiance to Christos.

adults.question: How does the Antioch church's famine relief (11:27-30) establish a pattern for inter-church relationships?
adults.option_a: The Antioch church's collection for Jerusalem establishes reciprocity: Jerusalem gave Antioch the gospel; Antioch gives Jerusalem material support. This pattern of spiritual and material sharing across ethnic and geographic lines becomes central to Paul's theology (Romans 15:26-27) and practice (the great collection, 2 Corinthians 8-9)
adults.option_b: It was a one-time emergency with no lasting impact
adults.option_c: Antioch was required to pay tribute to Jerusalem
adults.option_d: The relief was never actually delivered
adults.explanation: Acts 11:29-30 — "Then the disciples, every man according to his ability, determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judaea." This voluntary, proportional giving establishes the principle Paul later develops: "If the Gentiles have been made partakers of their spiritual things, their duty is also to minister unto them in carnal things" (Romans 15:27).

adults.question: What does "Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life" (11:18) imply about the nature of repentance?
adults.option_a: The Jerusalem church recognises repentance as a divine gift — God "grants" it, not merely demands it. Repentance unto life is something God gives (cf. 2 Timothy 2:25). This theological insight shapes the church's understanding: Gentile conversion is not human achievement but divine bestowal, just as with Jewish conversion
adults.option_b: Repentance is purely a human decision
adults.option_c: Only Jews can truly repent
adults.option_d: Repentance is unnecessary for Gentiles
adults.explanation: Acts 11:18 — "Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life." The verb "granted" (edoken = gave) presents repentance as God's gift. This matches Acts 5:31 ("to give repentance to Israel") and 2 Timothy 2:25 ("if God peradventure will give them repentance"). Salvation is grace-initiated from start to finish.

adults.question: What role does Barnabas play in the development of Pauline ministry?
adults.option_a: Barnabas is the catalyst for Paul's re-entry into active ministry — he sought Saul out in Tarsus (where Saul had been for perhaps a decade) and brought him to Antioch. Without Barnabas's initiative, Paul might have remained in obscurity. Barnabas's gift of encouragement and willingness to share leadership prepared the way for the Gentile mission
adults.option_b: Barnabas was Paul's superior throughout Acts
adults.option_c: Paul would have succeeded without any help
adults.option_d: Barnabas hindered Paul's ministry
adults.explanation: Acts 11:25-26 — "Then departed Barnabas to Tarsus, for to seek Saul." Barnabas had already vouched for Saul in Jerusalem (9:27). Now he retrieves him from Tarsus and gives him a year of co-ministry in Antioch. Barnabas consistently uses his reputation to open doors for others.

adults.question: How do the prophets mentioned in 11:27-28 function in the early church?
adults.option_a: New Testament prophets like Agabus provided Spirit-prompted revelation about future events and current situations. They served alongside teachers (cf. 13:1) as part of the church's leadership. Agabus's famine prediction led to concrete action — prophecy functioned not merely as prediction but as a catalyst for practical ministry and preparation
adults.option_b: Prophets had no role in the early church
adults.option_c: Agabus was the only prophet
adults.option_d: Prophets only repeated Old Testament passages
adults.explanation: Acts 11:27-28 — "And in these days came prophets from Jerusalem unto Antioch. And there stood up one of them named Agabus, and signified by the Spirit that there should be great dearth." Agabus reappears in 21:10-11. NT prophets complemented apostles and teachers, providing Spirit-directed insight that prompted practical response.

adults.question: What does the phrase "the hand of the Lord was with them" (11:21) signify about the Antioch mission?
adults.option_a: The "hand of the Lord" is an Old Testament expression for God's active power and favour (cf. 1 Kings 18:46, Ezra 7:9). Applied to the Antioch mission, it attributes the church's growth to divine action, not human strategy. "A great number believed, and turned unto the Lord" — the turning was empowered by God's hand, not merely by human eloquence
adults.option_b: The phrase is a casual expression with no deep meaning
adults.option_c: It refers to a literal hand appearing
adults.option_d: Only the apostles experienced God's hand
adults.explanation: Acts 11:21 — "And the hand of the Lord was with them: and a great number believed, and turned unto the Lord." The OT "hand of the Lord" denotes divine power in action (Exodus 9:3, Isaiah 59:1). Luke attributes Antioch's explosive growth not to methodology but to divine empowerment.

kids.question: What happened to Peter when he was in prison?
kids.option_a: An angel came at night, woke him up, and led him out of prison while the chains fell off his hands
kids.option_b: He dug a tunnel and escaped
kids.option_c: The guards let him go
kids.option_d: He climbed over the prison wall
kids.explanation: Acts 12:7-10 — "And, behold, the angel of the Lord came upon him, and a light shined in the prison: and he smote Peter on the side, and raised him up, saying, Arise up quickly. And his chains fell off from his hands."

kids.question: Who was praying for Peter while he was in prison?
kids.option_a: The church was praying without ceasing for him
kids.option_b: No one was praying
kids.option_c: Only his family prayed
kids.explanation: Acts 12:5 — "Peter therefore was kept in prison: but prayer was made without ceasing of the church unto God for him."

kids.question: What did the servant girl Rhoda do when she heard Peter's voice at the gate?
kids.option_a: She was so happy that she forgot to open the gate and ran to tell the others
kids.option_b: She opened the gate right away
kids.option_c: She screamed and ran away
kids.option_d: She did not recognise his voice
kids.explanation: Acts 12:13-14 — "And as Peter knocked at the door of the gate, a damsel came to hearken, named Rhoda. And when she knew Peter's voice, she opened not the gate for gladness, but ran in, and told how Peter stood before the gate."

teens.question: What did King Herod (Agrippa I) do to the apostle James?
teens.option_a: He killed James the brother of John with the sword
teens.option_b: He put James in prison for a year
teens.option_c: He banished James from Jerusalem
teens.option_d: He fined James a large sum of money
teens.explanation: Acts 12:1-2 — "Now about that time Herod the king stretched forth his hands to vex certain of the church. And he killed James the brother of John with the sword."

teens.question: Why did Herod arrest Peter after killing James?
teens.option_a: Because he saw it pleased the Jews, and he wanted to gain more popularity by persecuting Christians
teens.option_b: Peter had committed a crime against Rome
teens.option_c: Peter owed Herod money
teens.option_d: The apostles asked Herod to arrest Peter
teens.explanation: Acts 12:3 — "And because he saw it pleased the Jews, he proceeded further to take Peter also."

teens.question: How was Peter guarded in prison?
teens.option_a: He was chained between two soldiers, with guards at the door — four squads of four soldiers each
teens.option_b: He was in an unlocked room
teens.option_c: One guard watched the door
teens.option_d: He was in chains in a deep dungeon alone
teens.explanation: Acts 12:4,6 — "Delivered him to four quaternions of soldiers to keep him... Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains: and the keepers before the door kept the prison."

teens.question: What happened to King Herod when the people shouted that his voice was the voice of a god?
teens.option_a: An angel of the Lord smote him because he gave not God the glory, and he was eaten of worms and died
teens.option_b: He became the most powerful ruler in the world
teens.option_c: He was immediately crowned emperor
teens.option_d: Nothing happened and he lived many more years
teens.explanation: Acts 12:22-23 — "And the people gave a shout, saying, It is the voice of a god, and not of a man. And immediately the angel of the Lord smote him, because he gave not God the glory: and he was eaten of worms, and gave up the ghost."

teens.question: What did the believers say when Rhoda told them Peter was at the gate?
teens.option_a: They said she was mad — and when she kept insisting, they said it must be his angel
teens.option_b: They immediately ran to open the gate
teens.option_c: They did not believe because they were not praying
teens.option_d: They asked her to bring Peter inside
teens.explanation: Acts 12:15 — "And they said unto her, Thou art mad. But she constantly affirmed that it was even so. Then said they, It is his angel."

adults.question: Why does Luke juxtapose James's martyrdom and Peter's miraculous deliverance?
adults.option_a: The contrast demonstrates that God's sovereignty does not guarantee identical outcomes for all believers — James dies but Peter is delivered. Both were faithful apostles; the difference in outcome is divine prerogative, not a measure of faith or faithfulness. Luke shows that God's purposes sometimes include martyrdom and sometimes include deliverance, and both are within His sovereign will
adults.option_b: Luke is showing that Peter was more faithful than James
adults.option_c: James's death was a mistake by God
adults.option_d: Peter escaped through his own cleverness
adults.explanation: Acts 12:2,11 — James killed, Peter delivered. Luke offers no explanation for the different outcomes. This mirrors Hebrews 11:33-38 where some by faith "escaped the edge of the sword" and others by faith "were slain with the sword." Faith does not determine outcome; God does.

adults.question: What does the church praying "without ceasing" while Peter was in prison teach about prayer and God's sovereignty?
adults.option_a: The church prayed earnestly yet were shocked when their prayer was answered (v.15-16). This reveals that persistent prayer and imperfect faith can coexist — God answers prayer even when the pray-ers doubt. Their astonishment at the answer does not invalidate the prayer. God's faithfulness exceeds human faith
adults.option_b: The church's prayer had no effect
adults.option_c: Perfect faith is required for answered prayer
adults.option_d: The angel would have come without prayer
adults.explanation: Acts 12:5,15-16 — "Prayer was made without ceasing of the church unto God for him." Yet when Peter appears, "they said unto her, Thou art mad." The irony: they pray for deliverance but cannot believe it when it comes. God answers prayer despite the weakness of the pray-ers' faith.

adults.question: What does Herod Agrippa I's death by divine judgment reveal about the theme of opposition to God in Acts?
adults.option_a: Herod represents the ultimate futility of opposing God — he killed James, imprisoned Peter, and accepted divine worship. His gruesome death contrasts with the church's growth: "But the word of God grew and multiplied" (v.24). The pattern throughout Acts: opponents of the gospel are defeated while the word advances
adults.option_b: Herod's death was coincidental
adults.option_c: God does not judge political leaders
adults.option_d: Herod was punished for a minor offence
adults.explanation: Acts 12:23-24 — Herod "gave not God the glory: and he was eaten of worms, and gave up the ghost. But the word of God grew and multiplied." The juxtaposition is deliberate: the persecutor dies, the word lives. Josephus confirms Herod Agrippa's sudden death (Antiquities 19.8.2), corroborating Luke.

adults.question: What is the narrative significance of Peter's escape echoing the Passover/Exodus?
adults.option_a: The timing is during Passover (v.3-4, "after Easter" = after Passover). Peter is chained between soldiers (like Israel in Egypt), delivered at night by an angel (like the Passover angel), passes through gates that open of their own accord (like the Red Sea), and is freed from a tyrant (Herod, like Pharaoh). Luke patterns Peter's deliverance on the Exodus
adults.option_b: The Passover timing is coincidental
adults.option_c: There are no Exodus parallels
adults.option_d: Only the timing connects them
adults.explanation: Acts 12:3-11 — Passover context (v.3-4), night deliverance (v.6), angel strikes (v.7, like the death angel), chains fall (v.7, like bondage broken), iron gate opens automatically (v.10, like the sea parting). Luke's Exodus typology shows God's delivering power is the same across generations.

adults.question: What does Peter's initial confusion ("he wist not that it was true which was done by the angel; but thought he saw a vision," v.9) add to the narrative?
adults.option_a: Peter's confusion humanises the miraculous — even the apostle cannot immediately distinguish between vision and reality. It emphasises the angel's initiative: Peter is passive throughout the escape, simply following instructions. The deliverance is entirely divine — Peter contributes nothing but obedience. This underscores that salvation is God's work
adults.option_b: Peter was too sleepy to understand
adults.option_c: Angels always cause confusion
adults.option_d: Peter frequently confused visions with reality
adults.explanation: Acts 12:9,11 — Peter follows the angel's commands mechanically, unable to distinguish vision from reality. Only outside does he say: "Now I know of a surety, that the Lord hath sent his angel, and hath delivered me." The passive apostle and active angel dramatise divine initiative.

adults.question: How does Acts 12:24 function as a structural marker in the book?
adults.option_a: This is one of Luke's progress reports (cf. 6:7, 9:31, 16:5, 19:20, 28:31) that mark major narrative transitions. "The word of God grew and multiplied" concludes the Jerusalem section of Acts. After this, the focus shifts to the Antioch church and Paul's missionary journeys. The verb "grew" (euxanen) echoes the parable of the sower — the word is seed that cannot be suppressed
adults.option_b: Verse 24 is an insignificant summary
adults.option_c: It only applies to Jerusalem
adults.option_d: Luke accidentally repeated himself
adults.explanation: Acts 12:24 — "But the word of God grew and multiplied." This progress report closes the first half of Acts (Jerusalem-centred, Peter-focused). Chapter 13 begins the second half (Antioch-centred, Paul-focused). The word's growth contrasts with Herod's death — the persecutor falls, the gospel advances.

adults.question: What does the believers' suggestion that "it is his angel" (12:15) reveal about early Jewish-Christian belief?
adults.option_a: The response reflects a Jewish belief in guardian angels who could appear in the likeness of the person they guarded (cf. Matthew 18:10). The believers found it easier to believe Peter's angel appeared than that Peter himself was freed — revealing both their angel theology and their limited expectations of God's power despite their own prayers
adults.option_b: They literally thought Peter was dead and his ghost appeared
adults.option_c: Guardian angel beliefs were pagan, not Jewish
adults.option_d: They were joking about Rhoda's claim
adults.explanation: Acts 12:15 — "It is his angel." Jewish angelology included the concept of personal guardian angels (cf. Matthew 18:10, "their angels do always behold the face of my Father"). The believers' explanation reveals their theological framework: they believed in angels more readily than in answered prayer.

adults.question: How does Acts 12 close the "Peter section" of Acts and transition to the "Paul section"?
adults.option_a: After Peter's deliverance and departure to "another place" (v.17), he largely disappears from Acts (appearing only at the council in chapter 15). Herod's death removes the primary Jewish persecutor. Barnabas and Saul return from their relief mission (v.25). The stage is set for the Antioch church to commission the first missionary journey — the gospel goes to the Gentile world
adults.option_b: Peter remains the central figure throughout Acts
adults.option_c: Acts 12 has no transitional function
adults.option_d: Paul was already the main character
adults.explanation: Acts 12:17,25 — Peter goes to "another place" (likely outside Jerusalem, perhaps Antioch or Rome). Barnabas and Saul return to Antioch with John Mark. Chapter 13 opens with the Antioch church sending Barnabas and Saul. Luke masterfully transitions from Peter's story to Paul's story at the hinge of Acts.

kids.question: Who sent Barnabas and Saul on their first missionary journey?
kids.option_a: The Holy Ghost told the church at Antioch to send them
kids.option_b: The Roman emperor commanded them to go
kids.option_c: Peter asked them to travel
kids.explanation: Acts 13:2-4 — "The Holy Ghost said, Separate me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them... So they, being sent forth by the Holy Ghost, departed."

kids.question: What happened to a sorcerer named Elymas when he tried to stop Paul from preaching?
kids.option_a: He became blind for a time
kids.option_b: He ran away in fear
kids.option_c: He fell asleep
kids.explanation: Acts 13:11 — "And now, behold, the hand of the Lord is upon thee, and thou shalt be blind, not seeing the sun for a season. And immediately there fell on him a mist and a darkness."

kids.question: What did Paul and Barnabas tell the Jews who rejected the gospel?
kids.option_a: We turn to the Gentiles, for so the Lord has commanded us
kids.option_b: We will come back next week
kids.option_c: We are going home to Jerusalem
kids.option_d: We will ask the Romans for help
kids.explanation: Acts 13:46 — "Then Paul and Barnabas waxed bold, and said... seeing ye put it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life, lo, we turn to the Gentiles."

teens.question: What major transition occurs with Saul's name in chapter 13?
teens.option_a: Luke begins using "Paul" instead of "Saul" — his Roman name — marking the shift from the Jewish mission to the Gentile mission
teens.option_b: Saul changed his name because he was ashamed
teens.option_c: A Roman governor renamed him
teens.option_d: The church voted to change his name
teens.explanation: Acts 13:9 — "Then Saul, (who also is called Paul,) filled with the Holy Ghost..." From this point Luke uses "Paul." Jews commonly had both Hebrew and Roman/Greek names. The switch signals Paul's Gentile-focused ministry.

teens.question: What was the main point of Paul's sermon in the synagogue at Antioch in Pisidia?
teens.option_a: He traced Israel's history from the patriarchs to David, then proclaimed that Jesus is the promised Saviour — crucified, buried, and raised from the dead, and through Him forgiveness of sins is offered
teens.option_b: He told the people to follow the law of Moses more strictly
teens.option_c: He preached about the coming destruction of Rome
teens.option_d: He encouraged them to move to Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Acts 13:23,30,38 — "Of this man's seed hath God according to his promise raised unto Israel a Saviour, Jesus... But God raised him from the dead... through this man is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins."

teens.question: What Scripture did Paul quote to prove the resurrection of Jesus?
teens.option_a: Psalm 16:10 — "Thou shalt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption" — arguing that David died and decayed, but Jesus was raised and did not see corruption
teens.option_b: Genesis 1:1 about creation
teens.option_c: Exodus 20 about the Ten Commandments
teens.option_d: Proverbs 3 about trusting the Lord
teens.explanation: Acts 13:35-37 — "Wherefore he saith also in another psalm, Thou shalt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption. For David... fell on sleep, and was laid unto his fathers, and saw corruption: But he, whom God raised again, saw no corruption."

teens.question: How did the Gentiles react when Paul said he was turning to them?
teens.option_a: The Gentiles were glad and glorified the word of the Lord, and as many as were ordained to eternal life believed
teens.option_b: The Gentiles were angry and rejected Paul
teens.option_c: The Gentiles ignored Paul and walked away
teens.option_d: The Gentiles asked Paul to come back next year
teens.explanation: Acts 13:48 — "And when the Gentiles heard this, they were glad, and glorified the word of the Lord: and as many as were ordained to eternal life believed."

teens.question: What happened to Paul and Barnabas after the Jews stirred up persecution?
teens.option_a: They shook off the dust of their feet and went to Iconium, but the disciples were filled with joy and the Holy Ghost
teens.option_b: They returned to Jerusalem in defeat
teens.option_c: They gave up and stopped preaching
teens.option_d: They joined the synagogue to avoid trouble
teens.explanation: Acts 13:51-52 — "But they shook off the dust of their feet against them, and came unto Iconium. And the disciples were filled with joy, and with the Holy Ghost."

adults.question: How does the Antioch church's commissioning of Barnabas and Saul (13:1-4) establish a pattern for missionary sending?
adults.option_a: The commissioning combines prophetic direction ("the Holy Ghost said"), communal discernment (church leaders fasting and praying), and formal commission (laying on of hands). The mission is Spirit-initiated, church-confirmed, and community-supported — not an individual venture. This threefold pattern becomes the model for all subsequent missionary sending
adults.option_b: Barnabas and Saul decided on their own
adults.option_c: Only the apostles in Jerusalem could send missionaries
adults.option_d: The commissioning was a formality with no significance
adults.explanation: Acts 13:1-4 — "As they ministered to the Lord, and fasted, the Holy Ghost said, Separate me Barnabas and Saul." Three elements: Spirit's call (v.2), church's confirmation through prayer/fasting (v.3), and formal commissioning (laying on of hands, v.3). "Sent forth by the Holy Ghost" (v.4) — the Spirit is the primary sender.

adults.question: How does Paul's Antioch-in-Pisidia sermon (13:16-41) parallel Peter's Pentecost sermon while adapting it for a diaspora synagogue audience?
adults.option_a: Both follow the same kerygmatic structure: Israel's history leading to Jesus, His death and resurrection, Scripture proofs (both cite Psalm 16), and a call to respond. But Paul adapts by adding more historical review (patriarchs, exodus, judges, Saul, David), invoking "the children of the stock of Abraham" (v.26), and introducing justification language: "by him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses" (v.39)
adults.option_b: Paul's sermon is completely different from Peter's
adults.option_c: Both sermons are identical word for word
adults.option_d: Paul only quoted from the book of Moses
adults.explanation: Acts 13:16-41 — Paul's sermon mirrors Acts 2 in structure but adds the Abraham-to-David historical arc (vv.17-22) for a diaspora audience that valued patriarchal heritage. The justification statement (v.39) goes beyond Peter's sermon, introducing Pauline soteriology in embryonic form.

adults.question: What does "as many as were ordained to eternal life believed" (13:48) teach about election and faith?
adults.option_a: The verse presents divine election ("ordained" = tetagmenoi, meaning appointed/placed in order) and human faith ("believed") in a single sentence without resolving the tension. God's prior appointment and the human act of believing are both affirmed. Luke does not present this as contradictory but as complementary — those whom God appointed came to faith
adults.option_b: Only human decision determines salvation
adults.option_c: God forces people to believe against their will
adults.option_d: The verse is about political ordination
adults.explanation: Acts 13:48 — "As many as were ordained (tetagmenoi) to eternal life believed." The perfect passive participle "ordained" points to a prior divine action. Luke states the fact without philosophical explanation. Both sovereignty and faith are affirmed: God ordains, people believe. The tension is left unresolved.

adults.question: What is the significance of Paul's citation of Isaiah 49:6 ("a light of the Gentiles") in 13:47?
adults.option_a: Paul applies the Servant Song of Isaiah 49:6 — originally about Israel/the Servant — to his own Gentile mission. This is remarkable: Paul sees himself and Barnabas participating in the Servant's mission of bringing light to the nations. The church's mission is the continuation of the Servant's work — extending Christ's light to the ends of the earth
adults.option_b: Paul was misquoting Isaiah
adults.option_c: Isaiah 49 is only about the nation of Israel
adults.option_d: The citation was irrelevant to the situation
adults.explanation: Acts 13:47 — "For so hath the Lord commanded us, saying, I have set thee to be a light of the Gentiles, that thou shouldest be for salvation unto the ends of the earth." Paul applies Isaiah 49:6 (the Servant's commission) to his apostolic calling — identifying the church's Gentile mission with the Servant's universal vocation.

adults.question: How does Paul's statement about justification (13:39) anticipate his later epistolary theology?
adults.option_a: Paul introduces justification language unprecedented in Acts: "by him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses." This is embryonic Galatians/Romans theology — justification through faith in Christ accomplishes what the Mosaic law could not. It is Paul's earliest recorded articulation of justification by faith
adults.option_b: Paul never discussed justification in Acts
adults.option_c: This verse contradicts his later letters
adults.option_d: Justification language was common in all apostolic preaching
adults.explanation: Acts 13:39 — "And by him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses." This anticipates Romans 3:20-28 and Galatians 2:16. The law's inability and Christ's sufficiency are both stated. Luke shows Paul's theology emerging in his preaching before his letters.

adults.question: What does Paul's synagogue strategy (going to Jews first, then Gentiles) reveal about his missionary method?
adults.option_a: Paul consistently goes to the synagogue first in each city — not because the gospel belongs exclusively to Jews but because of covenant priority ("to the Jew first," Romans 1:16) and practical strategy (God-fearers in synagogues were receptive bridge populations). When Jews reject, Paul turns to Gentiles — a pattern repeated throughout his journeys
adults.option_b: Paul went to synagogues because Gentiles would not listen
adults.option_c: The synagogue strategy was accidental
adults.option_d: Paul only preached to Jews throughout Acts
adults.explanation: Acts 13:14,46 — Paul enters the synagogue, preaches, is rejected by some Jews, and turns to Gentiles. This pattern repeats in Iconium (14:1), Thessalonica (17:1-5), Corinth (18:4-6), and Ephesus (19:8-9). The theological rationale: Romans 1:16 "to the Jew first, and also to the Greek."

adults.question: What does Paul's application of Psalm 2:7 and Isaiah 55:3 to the resurrection reveal about his hermeneutic?
adults.option_a: Paul reads Psalm 2:7 ("Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee") as fulfilled in the resurrection — God's declaration of Jesus as Son is publicly enacted by raising Him from the dead. Isaiah 55:3 ("the sure mercies of David") is interpreted as the Davidic covenant promises being secured through an incorruptible risen Messiah. Both are eschatological-Christological readings
adults.option_b: Paul interpreted these texts literally only
adults.option_c: These psalms have no messianic meaning
adults.option_d: The resurrection is not connected to Old Testament prophecy
adults.explanation: Acts 13:33-34 — Paul cites Psalm 2:7 ("this day have I begotten thee") for the resurrection and Isaiah 55:3 ("the sure mercies of David") for the permanence of resurrection life. His hermeneutic: OT promises to David find their ultimate fulfilment in the risen Christ, not in David's mortal dynasty.

adults.question: How does Paul's warning from Habakkuk 1:5 (Acts 13:40-41) function in his sermon?
adults.option_a: Paul ends with a prophetic warning: Habakkuk warned Israel that God was doing something unbelievable (the Babylonian invasion) that sceptics would reject. Paul applies this to the gospel — God is again doing an "unbelievable" work (salvation through a crucified Messiah) and those who despise it will perish. The warning transforms the sermon from invitation to urgent ultimatum
adults.option_b: The Habakkuk quotation was irrelevant padding
adults.option_c: Paul was threatening military invasion
adults.option_d: The warning applied only to Habakkuk's original audience
adults.explanation: Acts 13:40-41 — "Beware therefore, lest that come upon you, which is spoken of in the prophets; Behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish: for I work a work in your days, a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though a man declare it unto you." The unbelievable work shifts from Babylon to the cross — scepticism brings the same judgment.

kids.question: What happened to a crippled man in Lystra when Paul saw that he had faith?
kids.option_a: Paul told him to stand up on his feet, and the man leaped and walked
kids.option_b: Paul gave him a pair of crutches
kids.option_c: The man slowly learned to walk over many weeks
kids.explanation: Acts 14:9-10 — "The same heard Paul speak: who stedfastly beholding him, and perceiving that he had faith to be healed, Said with a loud voice, Stand upright on thy feet. And he leaped and walked."

kids.question: What did the people of Lystra try to do to Paul and Barnabas after the healing?
kids.option_a: They tried to worship them as gods, calling Barnabas Jupiter and Paul Mercury
kids.option_b: They threw them a party
kids.option_c: They gave them gold and silver
kids.option_d: They asked them to become kings
kids.explanation: Acts 14:11-12 — "The gods are come down to us in the likeness of men. And they called Barnabas, Jupiter; and Paul, Mercurius, because he was the chief speaker."

kids.question: What did some angry people do to Paul in Lystra?
kids.option_a: They stoned him and dragged him out of the city thinking he was dead
kids.option_b: They put him in prison
kids.option_c: They chased him with swords
kids.explanation: Acts 14:19 — "And there came thither certain Jews from Antioch and Iconium, who persuaded the people, and, having stoned Paul, drew him out of the city, supposing he had been dead."

teens.question: How did Paul and Barnabas react when the people tried to worship them as gods?
teens.option_a: They tore their clothes and rushed into the crowd shouting "We also are men of like passions with you" and urged them to turn from vanities to the living God
teens.option_b: They accepted the worship graciously
teens.option_c: They quietly left the city
teens.option_d: They asked the people to worship God instead of them at the temple of Jupiter
teens.explanation: Acts 14:14-15 — "Which when the apostles, Barnabas and Paul, heard of, they rent their clothes, and ran in among the people, crying out, And saying, Sirs, why do ye these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and preach unto you that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God."

teens.question: What did Paul and Barnabas tell the new believers about entering the kingdom of God?
teens.option_a: We must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God
teens.option_b: The kingdom of God is easy and requires no sacrifice
teens.option_c: Tribulation is a sign of God's displeasure
teens.option_d: Only people without problems can enter the kingdom
teens.explanation: Acts 14:22 — "Confirming the souls of the disciples, and exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that we must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God."

teens.question: What did Paul and Barnabas do in every church they had started?
teens.option_a: They ordained elders in every church, prayed with fasting, and commended them to the Lord
teens.option_b: They stayed permanently in each city
teens.option_c: They left without appointing any leaders
teens.option_d: They asked each church to send money to Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Acts 14:23 — "And when they had ordained them elders in every church, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they believed."

teens.question: What did Paul do after being stoned and left for dead outside Lystra?
teens.option_a: As the disciples stood around him, he rose up and came back into the city, and the next day went with Barnabas to Derbe
teens.option_b: He was carried to Jerusalem for healing
teens.option_c: He stayed in bed for several months
teens.option_d: He returned home to Tarsus permanently
teens.explanation: Acts 14:20 — "Howbeit, as the disciples stood round about him, he rose up, and came into the city: and the next day he departed with Barnabas to Derbe."

teens.question: What report did Paul and Barnabas give when they returned to Antioch?
teens.option_a: They gathered the church together and reported all that God had done with them, and how He had opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles
teens.option_b: They complained about how difficult the journey had been
teens.option_c: They asked for more money and supplies
teens.option_d: They said the Gentiles were not interested in the gospel
teens.explanation: Acts 14:27 — "And when they were come, and had gathered the church together, they rehearsed all that God had done with them, and how he had opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles."

adults.question: How does Paul's speech at Lystra (14:15-17) differ from his synagogue sermons, and what does this reveal about contextualised evangelism?
adults.option_a: At Lystra, Paul addresses pagans with no knowledge of Scripture — so he starts with natural revelation: God as Creator, provider of rain and fruitful seasons, and sustainer of life. He makes no Old Testament citations. This shows Paul adapting his message to his audience: Jews hear Scripture; pagans hear creation theology. The core message (turn to the living God) is the same; the entry point differs
adults.option_b: Paul always preached the same way to everyone
adults.option_c: Paul abandoned Scripture when speaking to pagans
adults.option_d: The Lystra speech had no theological content
adults.explanation: Acts 14:15-17 — Paul appeals to "the living God, which made heaven, and earth, and the sea" and to God's providential witness through "rain from heaven, and fruitful seasons." Compare with the Athens speech (17:22-31) — both start with creation, not covenant. Paul models 1 Corinthians 9:22: "I am made all things to all men."

adults.question: What does the rapid swing from worship to stoning at Lystra reveal about the nature of popular acclaim?
adults.option_a: The same crowd that tried to worship Paul as a god was persuaded to stone him — showing the volatility and fickleness of popular opinion. Human praise is unreliable and dangerous. The episode echoes Jesus' triumphal entry followed by "Crucify him!" within a week. Paul later reflects: "If I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ" (Galatians 1:10)
adults.option_b: The crowd was different people
adults.option_c: Paul provoked the stoning intentionally
adults.option_d: Worship and violence are unrelated
adults.explanation: Acts 14:11-13 vs 14:19 — From "the gods have come down" to "having stoned Paul, drew him out of the city." The swing from adulation to murder took only outside agitators to catalyse. Luke shows that popular praise is worthless — the same voices that deify you can destroy you.

adults.question: What does "we must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God" (14:22) teach about the relationship between suffering and salvation?
adults.option_a: Tribulation is not an obstacle to the kingdom but the pathway through which believers enter it. Paul, freshly stoned at Lystra, teaches not prosperity but a theology of the cross — suffering is the normal Christian experience, not an aberration. This prepares new believers for persecution and prevents disillusionment when it comes
adults.option_b: Tribulation can be avoided with enough faith
adults.option_c: Only Paul was expected to suffer
adults.option_d: Suffering means God has abandoned the believer
