adults.explanation: Acts 14:22 — "We must (dei = divine necessity) through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God." Paul teaches this immediately after being stoned — his wounds are his credentials. The "must" (dei) makes tribulation a theological necessity, not an optional experience.

adults.question: What does the appointment of elders (14:23) reveal about Paul's ecclesiology?
adults.option_a: Paul established local leadership in every new church — he did not create permanent dependency on apostolic presence. Elders (presbuteroi) provided ongoing pastoral care. The combination of prayer, fasting, and commending to the Lord shows that church planting includes structural organisation, spiritual dependence, and eventual apostolic withdrawal. Healthy churches are self-governing under local leaders
adults.option_b: Paul stayed in each church permanently
adults.option_c: Elders were only appointed in Jerusalem
adults.option_d: New churches did not need organised leadership
adults.explanation: Acts 14:23 — "When they had ordained them elders in every church, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord." Paul's pattern: evangelise, establish, organise, entrust, depart. The elders are "commended to the Lord" — ultimate dependence is on Christ, not on the apostle.

adults.question: How does the Lystra healing parallel the Beautiful Gate healing in Acts 3?
adults.option_a: Both involve a man lame from birth, healed through faith by an apostle's command. Both lead to a crowd response (worship/amazement) and a theological speech correcting the crowd's misunderstanding. Both result in opposition. Luke draws deliberate parallels between Peter's ministry (Acts 3) and Paul's (Acts 14), validating Paul's apostolic authority as equal to Peter's
adults.option_b: The two healings are unrelated
adults.option_c: Only Peter performed genuine miracles
adults.option_d: Paul copied Peter's method
adults.explanation: Acts 14:8-10 parallels Acts 3:1-10: lame from birth (3:2/14:8), faith perceived (3:4/14:9), command to rise (3:6/14:10), leaping response (3:8/14:10). Luke validates Paul's apostleship by mirroring Peter's signs.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Paul's statement that God "suffered all nations to walk in their own ways" (14:16)?
adults.option_a: Paul acknowledges a period of divine forbearance when God allowed the nations to follow their own paths without the revelation given to Israel (cf. Romans 3:25, Acts 17:30). This is not divine approval of paganism but patient tolerance. Now, through the gospel, God is calling all nations to repentance — the era of overlooked ignorance is ending
adults.option_b: God approved of pagan worship
adults.option_c: The nations were punished for walking in their own ways
adults.option_d: This era of forbearance continues indefinitely
adults.explanation: Acts 14:16 — "Who in times past suffered all nations to walk in their own ways." Compare Acts 17:30: "The times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent." The forbearance era is ending — the gospel inaugurates universal accountability.

adults.question: How does the phrase "opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles" (14:27) summarise the first missionary journey?
adults.option_a: The metaphor of an "opened door" attributes the Gentile mission to God's initiative — God opened the door, not Paul. Faith is the entry point, not circumcision or Torah observance. This summary statement prepares for the Jerusalem Council (chapter 15) where the question of Gentile admission will be formally debated. The first journey established the practical precedent
adults.option_b: Paul opened the door through his eloquence
adults.option_c: The door was opened by Roman political support
adults.option_d: Faith was secondary to circumcision
adults.explanation: Acts 14:27 — "How he had opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles." The subject is God ("he"), not Paul. The instrument is faith, not law. This report to Antioch documents what will become evidence at the Jerusalem Council (15:4,12): God Himself has acted to include Gentiles through faith alone.

adults.question: What does Paul's resilience after the Lystra stoning (rising and re-entering the city) demonstrate about apostolic commitment?
adults.option_a: Paul's return to the very city that stoned him demonstrates extraordinary courage and commitment to the mission. He did not seek vengeance or flee permanently but continued ministering. His later reference to "once was I stoned" (2 Corinthians 11:25) shows this was not forgotten. Paul embodied his own teaching that tribulation is the pathway to the kingdom
adults.option_b: Paul returned because he forgot something
adults.option_c: The stoning was not actually painful
adults.option_d: Paul had nowhere else to go
adults.explanation: Acts 14:20 — "He rose up, and came into the city: and the next day he departed with Barnabas to Derbe." Paul re-enters the city that just tried to kill him. He then retraces his steps through Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch (v.21) — revisiting places of persecution to strengthen disciples. The mission matters more than personal safety.

kids.question: What big question did the church leaders meet in Jerusalem to discuss?
kids.option_a: Whether Gentile believers needed to be circumcised and follow the law of Moses to be saved
kids.option_b: Whether to build a bigger church building
kids.option_c: Whether Peter or Paul should be the leader
kids.option_d: Whether to move the church to Rome
kids.explanation: Acts 15:1,5-6 — "Except ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, ye cannot be saved... But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses."

kids.question: What did the church leaders decide about the Gentile believers?
kids.option_a: They should not be troubled with the law of Moses but should avoid idols, blood, things strangled, and fornication
kids.option_b: They must follow every rule of Moses
kids.option_c: They should become Jewish before becoming Christian
kids.option_d: They should build their own separate churches
kids.explanation: Acts 15:19-20 — "My sentence is, that we trouble not them, which from among the Gentiles are turned to God: But that we write unto them, that they abstain from pollutions of idols, and from fornication, and from things strangled, and from blood."

kids.question: Why did Paul and Barnabas have a disagreement after the Jerusalem council?
kids.option_a: They disagreed about whether to take John Mark on their next journey because he had left them before
kids.option_b: They argued about which road to take
kids.option_c: They could not agree on what to eat
kids.option_d: They both wanted to visit the same city first
kids.explanation: Acts 15:37-39 — "And Barnabas determined to take with them John, whose surname was Mark. But Paul thought not good to take him with them, who departed from them from Pamphylia, and went not with them to the work."

teens.question: What argument did Peter make at the Jerusalem Council?
teens.option_a: God gave the Holy Ghost to the Gentiles just as He did to us, making no distinction — why put a yoke on them that neither our fathers nor we were able to bear? We believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ we shall be saved, even as they
teens.option_b: Gentiles must follow the full law of Moses
teens.option_c: Only Jewish Christians deserve the Holy Spirit
teens.option_d: Peter sided with those demanding circumcision
teens.explanation: Acts 15:8-11 — "God, which knoweth the hearts, bare them witness, giving them the Holy Ghost, even as he did unto us; And put no difference between us and them, purifying their hearts by faith... we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ we shall be saved, even as they."

teens.question: What evidence did Paul and Barnabas present to the council?
teens.option_a: They declared all the miracles and wonders God had done among the Gentiles through them
teens.option_b: They showed letters from Roman officials supporting them
teens.option_c: They presented a list of Gentile donations
teens.option_d: They argued from their own authority as apostles
teens.explanation: Acts 15:12 — "Then all the multitude kept silence, and gave audience to Barnabas and Paul, declaring what miracles and wonders God had wrought among the Gentiles by them."

teens.question: What Old Testament prophecy did James quote to support Gentile inclusion?
teens.option_a: Amos 9:11-12 about rebuilding the tabernacle of David so that the rest of mankind may seek after the Lord, including all the Gentiles who are called by His name
teens.option_b: Genesis 12 about Abraham's calling
teens.option_c: Psalm 22 about the suffering Messiah
teens.option_d: Ezekiel 37 about the valley of dry bones
teens.explanation: Acts 15:16-17 — "After this I will return, and will build again the tabernacle of David, which is fallen down... That the residue of men might seek after the Lord, and all the Gentiles, upon whom my name is called."

teens.question: What was the result of the disagreement between Paul and Barnabas about John Mark?
teens.option_a: They separated — Barnabas took Mark and sailed to Cyprus, while Paul chose Silas and went through Syria and Cilicia
teens.option_b: They resolved the argument and stayed together
teens.option_c: They asked the church to decide for them
teens.option_d: Both of them retired from missionary work
teens.explanation: Acts 15:39-40 — "And the contention was so sharp between them, that they departed asunder one from the other: and so Barnabas took Mark, and sailed unto Cyprus; And Paul chose Silas, and departed."

teens.question: How was the council's decision communicated to the Gentile churches?
teens.option_a: They sent a letter with Judas and Silas to Antioch, saying "it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things"
teens.option_b: Peter personally visited every church
teens.option_c: They sent a messenger on horseback to Rome
teens.option_d: They posted the decision on the temple wall
teens.explanation: Acts 15:25,28 — "It seemed good unto us... to send chosen men unto you... For it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things."

adults.question: Why is the Jerusalem Council the most important ecclesiastical event in Acts?
adults.option_a: It formally resolved the most critical theological question in early Christianity: the terms of Gentile salvation. The decision that Gentiles are saved by grace through faith without requiring circumcision or Torah observance preserved the gospel of grace and prevented Christianity from becoming a Jewish sect. Every subsequent Pauline mission depends on this decision
adults.option_b: It was a minor administrative meeting
adults.option_c: The question had already been settled
adults.option_d: Only local churches were affected
adults.explanation: Acts 15 resolves the question that could have destroyed the church: must Gentiles become Jews to be saved? The unanimous answer — no — preserves the universal gospel. Without this decision, Christianity would have remained a subset of Judaism, and the Gentile mission would have been impossible.

adults.question: How does Peter's argument at the council ground Gentile inclusion in both experience and theology?
adults.option_a: Peter cites experience (God gave Gentiles the Spirit, 15:8), makes a theological argument (God purified their hearts by faith, not by law, 15:9), and concludes with a grace-alone soteriology (salvation is through grace for Jews and Gentiles equally, 15:11). Remarkably, Peter says Jews are saved "even as they" — putting Jewish salvation on Gentile terms, not vice versa
adults.option_b: Peter only used emotional arguments
adults.option_c: Peter argued for circumcision
adults.option_d: Peter's argument was rejected by the council
adults.explanation: Acts 15:8-11 — Peter's threefold argument: (1) Experiential: "God gave them the Holy Ghost, even as he did unto us" (v.8); (2) Theological: "purifying their hearts by faith" (v.9); (3) Soteriological: "through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ we shall be saved, even as they" (v.11). The last phrase is revolutionary — Jews saved like Gentiles, not vice versa.

adults.question: What are the four prohibitions in the apostolic decree (15:20,29), and why were these specific items chosen?
adults.option_a: The four prohibitions — food sacrificed to idols, blood, things strangled, and sexual immorality — represent either Noahide laws (Genesis 9) binding on all humanity, or Leviticus 17-18 requirements for Gentiles living among Israel. They are minimum requirements for table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers, not salvation requirements. They preserved community unity
adults.option_b: They are the minimum requirements for salvation
adults.option_c: They were randomly selected
adults.option_d: Only sexual immorality had moral significance
adults.explanation: Acts 15:20,29 — The four items correspond to Leviticus 17-18 regulations for resident aliens (ger): idolatry (Lev 17:8-9), blood consumption (Lev 17:10-12), strangled animals (Lev 17:13-14), and sexual immorality (Lev 18). These enabled Jewish-Gentile table fellowship — the practical expression of theological unity.

adults.question: What does the phrase "it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us" (15:28) reveal about apostolic decision-making?
adults.option_a: The phrase claims divine-human cooperation in the council's decision — the Holy Spirit's guidance working through communal deliberation. The council did not receive a direct oracle but discerned the Spirit's direction through testimony (Peter, Paul, Barnabas), Scripture (James citing Amos), and communal agreement. This establishes a model for Spirit-led institutional decision-making
adults.option_b: The Holy Spirit dictated the letter word for word
adults.option_c: The phrase was diplomatic rhetoric with no spiritual reality
adults.option_d: Only James made the decision
adults.explanation: Acts 15:28 — "It seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us." This remarkable formula combines Spirit and community. The process included: apostolic testimony (Peter, v.7-11), missionary evidence (Paul/Barnabas, v.12), scriptural argument (James, v.13-21), and communal agreement (v.22,25). The Spirit works through, not apart from, deliberation.

adults.question: How does James's use of Amos 9:11-12 support the council's decision?
adults.option_a: James quotes Amos's prophecy that God will rebuild David's fallen tabernacle so that "the residue of men might seek after the Lord, and all the Gentiles, upon whom my name is called." James argues that Gentile inclusion was always part of God's prophetic plan — it is not an innovation but a fulfilment. The rebuilt Davidic kingdom includes Gentiles by divine design
adults.option_b: James misquoted Amos
adults.option_c: Amos was speaking only about Israel's national restoration
adults.option_d: The quotation contradicted the council's decision
adults.explanation: Acts 15:15-18 — James says "to this agree the words of the prophets" and quotes Amos 9:11-12 (LXX). The key: Gentiles are "called by my name" — they belong to God as Gentiles, not as converts to Judaism. James provides the scriptural warrant for what Peter described experientially and Paul demonstrated practically.

adults.question: What does the Paul-Barnabas separation (15:36-41) teach about conflict in Christian ministry?
adults.option_a: Even Spirit-filled leaders can sharply disagree — the "contention was so sharp" (paroxusmos) that they separated. Yet Luke does not condemn either party. The result was two mission teams instead of one (Paul-Silas to Asia Minor; Barnabas-Mark to Cyprus). God's sovereignty can use even human conflict to advance the mission. Paul later reconciles with Mark (2 Timothy 4:11)
adults.option_b: The separation was a catastrophic failure
adults.option_c: Paul was completely right and Barnabas was wrong
adults.option_d: Barnabas abandoned the mission entirely
adults.explanation: Acts 15:39 — "The contention was so sharp between them, that they departed asunder." The word paroxusmos is strong (it gives us "paroxysm"). Yet providence multiplies the mission: two teams cover more ground. Paul's later affirmation of Mark (Colossians 4:10, 2 Timothy 4:11) shows eventual reconciliation.

adults.question: How does the Jerusalem Council balance continuity with Judaism and freedom in Christ?
adults.option_a: The council maintains continuity by not abolishing the law for Jewish believers (James still values Moses, v.21) while granting freedom to Gentile believers. The four prohibitions are minimal boundary markers that enable fellowship without imposing full Torah observance. The result is a "third way" — neither pure Torah observance nor lawless antinomianism, but grace-based community with practical love
adults.option_b: The council abolished all Jewish law
adults.option_c: The council required full Torah observance for all
adults.option_d: No compromise was reached
adults.explanation: Acts 15:19-21 — James's "sentence" (krinein = judgment) treads carefully: "trouble not" Gentiles (freedom), but observe four practices (community sensitivity), because "Moses... hath in every city them that preach him" (awareness of Jewish sensibilities). The decree balances theological freedom with relational wisdom.

adults.question: What is the significance of the letter being addressed to "the brethren which are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia" (15:23)?
adults.option_a: The address acknowledges Gentile believers as "brethren" — full siblings in the faith, not second-class members. The geographic scope (Antioch, Syria, Cilicia) targets the regions where the circumcision controversy was most acute. The letter format — a formal decree from Jerusalem — gives the decision institutional authority and provides Gentile churches with a written document to counter Judaising pressure
adults.option_b: The letter was a private note with no authority
adults.option_c: Only Antioch was addressed
adults.option_d: The term "brethren" excluded Gentiles
adults.explanation: Acts 15:23 — "The apostles and elders and brethren send greeting unto the brethren which are of the Gentiles." Calling Gentiles "brethren" is theologically loaded — it affirms their full membership in the covenant community. The letter provides written, authoritative backing for Gentile freedom.

kids.question: What young man did Paul invite to join his missionary team?
kids.option_a: Timothy, whose mother was a Jewish believer
kids.option_b: John Mark, who had left them before
kids.option_c: Barnabas, his old partner
kids.explanation: Acts 16:1-3 — "Then came he to Derbe and Lystra: and, behold, a certain disciple was there, named Timotheus, the son of a certain woman, which was a Jewess, and believed... Him would Paul have to go forth with him."

kids.question: What happened to Paul and Silas in prison at Philippi at midnight?
kids.option_a: They were praying and singing hymns to God, and suddenly a great earthquake shook the prison and all the doors opened
kids.option_b: They fell asleep peacefully
kids.option_c: The guards let them go quietly
kids.option_d: A fire broke out and they escaped
kids.explanation: Acts 16:25-26 — "And at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises unto God... And suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken: and immediately all the doors were opened, and every one's bands were loosed."

kids.question: What did the Philippian jailer ask Paul and Silas?
kids.option_a: Sirs, what must I do to be saved?
kids.option_b: How did you open the prison doors?
kids.option_c: Why were you singing so loudly?
kids.option_d: Can you teach me to sing?
kids.explanation: Acts 16:30 — "And brought them out, and said, Sirs, what must I do to be saved?"

teens.question: How did God direct Paul to go to Macedonia?
teens.option_a: Paul had a vision at night of a man from Macedonia pleading "Come over into Macedonia, and help us"
teens.option_b: An angel appeared in the temple
teens.option_c: A letter arrived from Macedonia
teens.option_d: Paul had always planned to go there
teens.explanation: Acts 16:9 — "And a vision appeared to Paul in the night; There stood a man of Macedonia, and prayed him, saying, Come over into Macedonia, and help us."

teens.question: Who was Lydia, and how did she become a believer?
teens.option_a: A seller of purple cloth from Thyatira who worshipped God — the Lord opened her heart to respond to Paul's message, and she and her household were baptised
teens.option_b: A Roman official who invited Paul to her palace
teens.option_c: A Jewish priestess at the temple
teens.option_d: A slave girl who served in a household
teens.explanation: Acts 16:14-15 — "And a certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple, of the city of Thyatira, which worshipped God, heard us: whose heart the Lord opened, that she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul. And when she was baptized, and her household."

teens.question: Why were Paul and Silas thrown into prison at Philippi?
teens.option_a: They cast a spirit of divination out of a slave girl, and her masters were angry because they lost their source of income from her fortune-telling
teens.option_b: They preached without a Roman permit
teens.option_c: They refused to pay taxes to the city
teens.option_d: They caused a riot in the marketplace
teens.explanation: Acts 16:16-19 — "A certain damsel possessed with a spirit of divination... which brought her masters much gain by soothsaying... And when her masters saw that the hope of their gains was gone, they caught Paul and Silas, and drew them into the marketplace unto the rulers."

teens.question: What answer did Paul and Silas give to the jailer's question about being saved?
teens.option_a: Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house
teens.option_b: Follow the law of Moses perfectly
teens.option_c: Give all your money to the poor
teens.option_d: Do good deeds every day of your life
teens.explanation: Acts 16:31 — "And they said, Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house."

teens.question: Why did Paul refuse to leave prison quietly the next morning?
teens.option_a: Because he and Silas were Roman citizens who had been beaten and imprisoned publicly without a trial — Paul demanded that the magistrates come personally to release them
teens.option_b: He was too tired to leave early
teens.option_c: He wanted to finish preaching to the prisoners
teens.option_d: He did not trust the guards to let them go safely
teens.explanation: Acts 16:37 — "They have beaten us openly uncondemned, being Romans, and have cast us into prison; and now do they thrust us out privily? nay verily; but let them come themselves and fetch us out."

adults.question: What does the Macedonian vision (16:9-10) reveal about divine guidance in mission?
adults.option_a: The vision comes after the Spirit had blocked Paul twice (forbidden by the Holy Ghost in Asia, v.6; the Spirit suffered them not in Bithynia, v.7). God guides through both negative constraints (closed doors) and positive direction (the vision). Paul's obedience to the vision brings the gospel to Europe for the first time — a pivotal moment in world history
adults.option_b: Paul planned the entire route himself
adults.option_c: The vision was just a dream with no significance
adults.option_d: The Spirit never blocked Paul's plans
adults.explanation: Acts 16:6-10 — Two divine blockages (v.6-7) followed by a positive vision (v.9). The "we" narrative begins at v.10 (Luke joins the team). God's guidance is both negative (closed doors) and positive (open doors). The result: the gospel enters Europe — arguably the most consequential geographic move in church history.

adults.question: What does the phrase "whose heart the Lord opened" (16:14) teach about the nature of conversion?
adults.option_a: Luke attributes Lydia's response to divine initiative — "the Lord opened her heart." While Lydia was already a God-fearer who "worshipped God," she needed the Lord to open her heart to respond to Paul's specific message about Jesus. Conversion requires both the proclaimed word (Paul's preaching) and the Lord's inner work (opening the heart). Human response follows divine initiative
adults.option_b: Lydia opened her own heart through study
adults.option_c: The phrase is merely poetic
adults.option_d: Heart-opening was a medical procedure
adults.explanation: Acts 16:14 — "Whose heart the Lord opened, that she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul." The verb "opened" (dienoixen) is the same word used for opening the Scriptures (Luke 24:32) and opening understanding (Luke 24:45). God illuminates the heart so that the word can be received.

adults.question: How do the three Philippian conversions (Lydia, slave girl, jailer) illustrate the gospel's universal reach?
adults.option_a: The three converts represent a cross-section of Roman society: Lydia (wealthy businesswoman, Asian God-fearer), the slave girl (lowest social class, demon-possessed), and the jailer (Roman military middle class, pagan). The gospel reaches every stratum: rich and poor, free and slave, male and female, Jew-adjacent and pagan. Philippi is a microcosm of the universal gospel
adults.option_b: Only Lydia's conversion was important
adults.option_c: The three conversions are unrelated
adults.option_d: Paul targeted only wealthy converts
adults.explanation: Acts 16:14,18,33 — Lydia (wealthy, religious, female, Asian); slave girl (powerless, exploited, female, possessed); jailer (Roman authority, male, pagan). Three conversions spanning the entire social spectrum demonstrate that the gospel is "the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth" (Romans 1:16).

adults.question: What is the significance of Paul asserting his Roman citizenship after the beating (16:37)?
adults.option_a: Paul's appeal to citizenship serves a strategic purpose: by demanding a public apology, he protects the fledgling Philippian church from future persecution. The magistrates' fear of having violated a citizen's rights ("they feared, when they heard that they were Romans," v.38) creates legal precedent that shields the believers. Paul uses legal tools for the church's benefit, not personal vengeance
adults.option_b: Paul was seeking revenge for his beating
adults.option_c: Roman citizenship had no legal significance
adults.option_d: Paul made up his citizenship
adults.explanation: Acts 16:37-39 — By forcing the magistrates to acknowledge their illegal action (beating/imprisoning Roman citizens without trial violated the Lex Valeria and Lex Porcia), Paul establishes that the church is not a criminal organisation. The magistrates' embarrassment provides a measure of protection for Lydia's house church.

adults.question: What does "Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved" (16:31) mean as the most concise gospel summary in Acts?
adults.option_a: This is the apostolic gospel distilled to its essence: the object of faith (the Lord Jesus Christ — divine Lord, historical Jesus, anointed Messiah), the nature of response (believe — personal trust, not mere intellectual assent), and the promise (thou shalt be saved — complete salvation). "And thy house" extends the promise to the household, anticipating household baptism (v.33)
adults.option_b: Belief is merely intellectual agreement
adults.option_c: Salvation requires additional works
adults.option_d: Only the jailer could be saved
adults.explanation: Acts 16:31 — The threefold title "Lord Jesus Christ" packs maximum Christology: Lord (divine authority), Jesus (human name, "God saves"), Christ (messianic office). "Believe" (pisteuson) is aorist imperative — a decisive act of trust. "Thou shalt be saved" is future passive — God effects the salvation. Grace from start to finish.

adults.question: What does Paul and Silas singing hymns at midnight in prison (16:25) reveal about the relationship between worship and suffering?
adults.option_a: Worship in suffering demonstrates faith that transcends circumstances — Paul and Silas praise God not because of their situation but despite it. Their worship transforms the prison into a sanctuary. The other prisoners "heard them" (v.25) — suffering-worship becomes a witness. The earthquake follows the worship, suggesting that praise releases divine power
adults.option_b: They sang to stay awake
adults.option_c: Singing was a Roman prison custom
adults.option_d: The hymns were a distraction technique
adults.explanation: Acts 16:25 — "And at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises unto God: and the prisoners heard them." Worship in chains echoes Psalm 42 ("Why art thou cast down?") and anticipates Paul's later prison epistles (Philippians, written from this very city's church). The prisoners' listening makes worship a form of witness.

adults.question: How does the "we" section beginning at 16:10 affect the historical credibility of Acts?
adults.option_a: The shift to first-person plural ("we") indicates Luke joined the team at Troas. These "we passages" (16:10-17, 20:5-15, 21:1-18, 27:1-28:16) signal eyewitness participation, significantly strengthening Acts' historical reliability. Luke transitions from secondary reporting to personal testimony, providing a contemporaneous account of events he witnessed firsthand
adults.option_b: The "we" is an editorial convention with no personal meaning
adults.option_c: Luke was present throughout all of Acts
adults.option_d: Someone else wrote the "we" sections
adults.explanation: Acts 16:10 — "Immediately we endeavoured to go into Macedonia." The sudden shift from "they" to "we" indicates the author (Luke) joining the missionary team at Troas. The "we" sections are among the strongest evidence for Lukan authorship and eyewitness testimony in Acts.

adults.question: What does the casting out of the slave girl's spirit reveal about the relationship between truth and demonic testimony?
adults.option_a: The spirit spoke truth — "these men are the servants of the most high God, which shew unto us the way of salvation" (v.17) — yet Paul was grieved and cast it out. This reveals that truth from demonic sources is still unacceptable because it creates false association between the gospel and the occult. Jesus similarly silenced demons who proclaimed His identity (Mark 1:24-25). The gospel needs no demonic endorsement
adults.option_b: Paul should have accepted the free advertising
adults.option_c: The spirit was helping the mission
adults.option_d: Demons always lie
adults.explanation: Acts 16:17-18 — "These men are the servants of the most high God." True words, wrong source. Paul was "grieved" (diaponeomai = deeply troubled). Like Jesus (Mark 1:25,34), Paul refuses demonic testimony because association with the occult contaminates the gospel's credibility. Source matters as much as content.

kids.question: What did the people of Berea do when Paul preached to them?
kids.option_a: They searched the Scriptures daily to see whether what Paul said was true
kids.option_b: They immediately threw Paul out
kids.option_c: They fell asleep during his sermon
kids.option_d: They asked Paul to come back in a year
kids.explanation: Acts 17:11 — "These were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, and searched the scriptures daily, whether those things were so."

kids.question: What did Paul notice in the city of Athens?
kids.option_a: An altar with the inscription "TO THE UNKNOWN GOD"
kids.option_b: A beautiful garden with many flowers
kids.option_c: A very tall tower reaching to the sky
kids.option_d: A golden statue of a king
kids.explanation: Acts 17:23 — "For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD."

kids.question: What did Paul say God made from one blood?
kids.option_a: All nations of men to dwell on all the face of the earth
kids.option_b: Only the people of Israel
kids.option_c: Only the people of Greece
kids.explanation: Acts 17:26 — "And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth."

teens.question: Why were the Bereans called "more noble" than the Thessalonians?
teens.option_a: They received the word with all readiness of mind and searched the Scriptures daily to verify what Paul taught — they combined openness with discernment
teens.option_b: They were wealthier and more educated
teens.option_c: They gave Paul more money
teens.option_d: They built him a synagogue to preach in
teens.explanation: Acts 17:11 — "These were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, and searched the scriptures daily, whether those things were so."

teens.question: What two groups of Greek philosophers debated with Paul in Athens?
teens.option_a: The Epicureans and the Stoics
teens.option_b: The Platonists and the Aristotelians
teens.option_c: The Cynics and the Sceptics
teens.option_d: The Pythagoreans and the Sophists
teens.explanation: Acts 17:18 — "Then certain philosophers of the Epicureans, and of the Stoicks, encountered him."

teens.question: What did Paul say God now commands all men everywhere to do?
teens.option_a: Repent — because He hath appointed a day in which He will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom He hath ordained, and He proved it by raising Him from the dead
teens.option_b: Build temples in every city
teens.option_c: Study Greek philosophy
teens.option_d: Offer sacrifices to the unknown god
teens.explanation: Acts 17:30-31 — "And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that he hath raised him from the dead."

teens.question: What accusation did the Thessalonian Jews make against Paul and Silas?
teens.option_a: These men who have turned the world upside down have come here also, saying there is another king, one Jesus
teens.option_b: They were selling illegal goods
teens.option_c: They were Roman spies
teens.option_d: They were stealing from the synagogue
teens.explanation: Acts 17:6-7 — "These that have turned the world upside down are come hither also... and these all do contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, one Jesus."

teens.question: How did the Athenians react when Paul spoke about the resurrection of the dead?
teens.option_a: Some mocked, some said "We will hear thee again," and some believed, including Dionysius and a woman named Damaris
teens.option_b: Everyone believed immediately
teens.option_c: They arrested Paul and threw him in prison
teens.option_d: They all mocked and Paul fled the city
teens.explanation: Acts 17:32-34 — "And when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked: and others said, We will hear thee again of this matter... Howbeit certain men clave unto him, and believed: among the which was Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris."

adults.question: How does Paul's Areopagus speech (17:22-31) demonstrate a masterful contextualised approach to a pagan audience?
adults.option_a: Paul engages Athens on its own terms: he begins with their religiosity (the unknown god altar), quotes their own poets (Aratus and possibly Cleanthes — "in him we live, and move, and have our being"), yet subverts pagan assumptions by proclaiming God as Creator, Judge, and resurrector. He builds bridges through common ground but drives toward the distinctly Christian claims of resurrection and judgment
adults.option_b: Paul ignored Athenian culture entirely
adults.option_c: He simply repeated his synagogue sermon
adults.option_d: The speech compromised the gospel
adults.explanation: Acts 17:22-31 — Paul's method: observation (altar, v.23), common ground (God as Creator, v.24-26), pagan poets as pre-evangelistic witnesses (v.28), challenge to idolatry (v.29), and the uniquely Christian proclamation: repentance, judgment, and resurrection (vv.30-31). He is all things to all men without compromising content.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Paul's declaration that God "dwelleth not in temples made with hands" (17:24)?
adults.option_a: Paul echoes Stephen's argument (Acts 7:48) and Isaiah 66:1-2 to challenge both Jewish temple-centrism and Greek temple religion. The transcendent Creator cannot be contained in human constructions. This is both a bridge (Stoics agreed God pervaded all things) and a challenge (Epicureans separated gods from human affairs). Paul affirms both divine transcendence and immanence
adults.option_b: Paul was only criticising Jewish worship
adults.option_c: Greek temples were acceptable to God
adults.option_d: Paul rejected all forms of worship
adults.explanation: Acts 17:24-25 — "God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands; Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing." This challenges every religious system that domesticates God — Jewish, Greek, or otherwise.

adults.question: How does the "unknown god" altar function in Paul's argument?
adults.option_a: Paul uses the altar as a point of contact — the Athenians' own acknowledgment of ignorance becomes the opening for revelation. He does not say they were already worshipping the true God unknowingly but rather: "Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you." He repurposes their confession of ignorance as a platform for the gospel of the known God
adults.option_b: Paul was approving of idol worship
adults.option_c: The Athenians were already Christians
adults.option_d: The altar had no rhetorical significance
adults.explanation: Acts 17:23 — "Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you." Paul's genius: the altar proves Athens knows it does not know enough. Paul offers to fill the gap — not by affirming their worship but by replacing ignorance with revelation. It's a bridge, not an endorsement.

adults.question: What does Paul's quotation of pagan poets ("in him we live, and move, and have our being... we are also his offspring") accomplish?
adults.option_a: Paul cites Epimenides of Crete and Aratus of Cilicia (Paul's home region) to establish common ground — pagans themselves intuited truths about divine immanence and human dignity. By quoting their own authorities, Paul shows that general revelation confirms what special revelation explicitly teaches. Yet he radically reinterprets these insights in a monotheistic, creationist framework
adults.option_b: Paul was endorsing paganism
adults.option_c: The quotations are from the Old Testament
adults.option_d: Paul invented these quotes himself
adults.explanation: Acts 17:28 — "For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring." Paul does not cite the poets as authoritative but as witnesses to general revelation — even pagans glimpsed truths about God. He then redirects: if we are God's offspring, we should not worship gold/silver/stone images (v.29).

adults.question: What does "the times of this ignorance God winked at" (17:30) mean theologically?
adults.option_a: God exercised patient forbearance during the era before the gospel was widely proclaimed — He "overlooked" (huperidein) pagan ignorance in the sense of withholding immediate judgment, not approving of it. But "now" — with the resurrection as proof and the gospel being proclaimed — this era of forbearance ends. Universal accountability has arrived. All people everywhere are called to repent
adults.option_b: God approved of pagan worship in the past
adults.option_c: Ignorance permanently excuses sin
adults.option_d: The times of ignorance have not ended
adults.explanation: Acts 17:30 — "The times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent." "Winked at" (huperidein) means overlooked/passed over without full punishment — not approved. Compare Romans 3:25: God "passed over" former sins. The resurrection inaugurates a new epoch of universal accountability.

adults.question: What does "hath made of one blood all nations" (17:26) establish about human unity and dignity?
adults.option_a: Paul affirms the common origin of all humanity — all nations derive from one source (Adam). This was counter-cultural in a society that classified people by ethnicity (Greek vs barbarian). It undergirds both human equality and universal accountability. If all humans share one origin, all share one Creator and one coming Judge. The doctrine of creation grounds the ethics of universal dignity
adults.option_b: Paul was discussing blood types
adults.option_c: Only Greeks were made from one blood
adults.option_d: This was a common Greek philosophical idea
adults.explanation: Acts 17:26 — "Hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth." Against Greek ethnocentrism and Roman imperialism, Paul declares universal human kinship rooted in creation. This doctrine — all people from one ancestor — levels all ethnic hierarchies and establishes universal accountability before one God.

adults.question: Why did the resurrection provoke mockery from the Athenian philosophers?
adults.option_a: Greek philosophy generally viewed the body negatively — Plato taught that the soul's escape from the body was salvation. The idea of bodily resurrection was therefore philosophically absurd to Greeks. Epicureans denied any afterlife; Stoics believed in the soul's reabsorption into the cosmos. Bodily resurrection contradicted both schools. The gospel's scandal was not moral but metaphysical
adults.option_b: Greeks were familiar with resurrection and found it boring
adults.option_c: Only uneducated people mocked
adults.option_d: The resurrection was rejected because of translation issues
adults.explanation: Acts 17:32 — "When they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked." Greek dualism (body = prison, soul = divine) made resurrection absurd. Aeschylus had declared: "When the dust has drained the blood of a man, once he is slain, there is no resurrection." Paul's proclamation cut against the deepest assumptions of Hellenistic thought.

adults.question: How do the varied responses in Athens (mocking, postponing, believing) typify responses to the gospel throughout Acts?
adults.option_a: Luke presents three categories of response: outright rejection ("some mocked"), indecision/deferral ("we will hear thee again"), and faith ("certain men clave unto him, and believed"). These three responses recur throughout Acts and mirror the parable of the sower — hard ground, shallow ground, and good ground. Luke does not present Athens as a failure but as a realistic picture of gospel reception
adults.option_b: Athens was a complete failure for Paul
adults.option_c: Everyone in Athens eventually believed
adults.option_d: Luke only recorded the positive response
adults.explanation: Acts 17:32-34 — Three responses: mockery (hard hearts), delay (divided hearts), faith (open hearts). This pattern appears in every city: some believe, some reject, some defer. Luke's realism prevents both triumphalism and despair — the gospel always divides, and even small harvests (Dionysius, Damaris) are genuine victories.

kids.question: What job did Paul, Aquila, and Priscilla share?
kids.option_a: They were tentmakers
kids.option_b: They were fishermen
kids.option_c: They were carpenters
kids.explanation: Acts 18:3 — "And because he was of the same craft, he abode with them, and wrought: for by their occupation they were tentmakers."

kids.question: What did the Lord tell Paul in a night vision in Corinth?
kids.option_a: Be not afraid, but speak and hold not thy peace: for I am with thee, and no man shall hurt thee
kids.option_b: Leave this city immediately
kids.option_c: Build a church on this spot
kids.explanation: Acts 18:9-10 — "Then spake the Lord to Paul in the night by a vision, Be not afraid, but speak, and hold not thy peace: For I am with thee, and no man shall set on thee to hurt thee: for I have much people in this city."

kids.question: Who was Apollos?
kids.option_a: A man from Alexandria who was eloquent and mighty in the Scriptures
kids.option_b: A Roman soldier who became a Christian
kids.option_c: A fisherman from Galilee
kids.option_d: A priest from the temple in Jerusalem
kids.explanation: Acts 18:24 — "And a certain Jew named Apollos, born at Alexandria, an eloquent man, and mighty in the scriptures, came to Ephesus."

teens.question: Why did Aquila and Priscilla come to Corinth?
teens.option_a: Emperor Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome
teens.option_b: They were sent as missionaries by the Jerusalem church
teens.option_c: They came to trade purple cloth
teens.option_d: They were fleeing from persecution in Ephesus
teens.explanation: Acts 18:2 — "And found a certain Jew named Aquila, born in Pontus, lately come from Italy, with his wife Priscilla; (because that Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome)."

teens.question: What did Paul declare when the Jews in Corinth opposed him?
teens.option_a: Your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean: from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles
teens.option_b: I will continue preaching only in the synagogue
teens.option_c: I will ask the Romans to force you to listen
teens.option_d: I give up and am going home to Tarsus
teens.explanation: Acts 18:6 — "And when they opposed themselves, and blasphemed, he shook his raiment, and said unto them, Your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean: from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles."

teens.question: How long did Paul stay in Corinth?
teens.option_a: A year and six months, teaching the word of God among them
teens.option_b: Three months
teens.option_c: One week
teens.option_d: Five years
teens.explanation: Acts 18:11 — "And he continued there a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them."

teens.question: What did Gallio the Roman proconsul decide about the Jews' complaint against Paul?
teens.option_a: He refused to judge the matter, saying it was about Jewish law and not a Roman crime — and drove them from the judgment seat
teens.option_b: He sentenced Paul to prison
teens.option_c: He ordered Paul to leave Corinth
teens.option_d: He became a Christian and helped Paul
teens.explanation: Acts 18:14-16 — "If it were a matter of wrong or wicked lewdness, O ye Jews, reason would that I should bear with you: But if it be a question of words and names, and of your law, look ye to it; for I will be no judge of such matters. And he drave them from the judgment seat."

teens.question: What did Priscilla and Aquila do for Apollos?
teens.option_a: They took him aside and expounded unto him the way of God more perfectly — teaching him about Jesus and baptism in the Holy Spirit
teens.option_b: They gave him money to start a church
teens.option_c: They sent him back to Alexandria
teens.option_d: They asked him to stop preaching
teens.explanation: Acts 18:26 — "And he began to speak boldly in the synagogue: whom when Aquila and Priscilla had heard, they took him unto them, and expounded unto him the way of God more perfectly."

adults.question: What does Paul's tentmaking ministry (18:3) reveal about his philosophy of mission?
adults.option_a: Paul worked to support himself to avoid being a financial burden and to model self-sufficiency (cf. 1 Thessalonians 2:9, 2 Thessalonians 3:8). His manual labour also gave him natural access to the marketplace and credibility as a working man. He chose financial independence to remove any accusation of preaching for profit and to provide a model for new believers
adults.option_b: Paul was forced to work because no one supported him
adults.option_c: Tentmaking was his primary calling
adults.option_d: Manual labour was disgraceful for a rabbi
adults.explanation: Acts 18:3 — Paul worked as a tentmaker (skenopoios — likely leather-working). He later defended this practice in 1 Corinthians 9:12-15 and 2 Corinthians 11:7-9. It was strategic: financial independence, marketplace access, and modelling work ethic for converts from cultures that devalued manual labour.

adults.question: What does the Lord's night vision to Paul (18:9-10) reveal about the human side of apostolic ministry?
adults.option_a: The vision implies Paul was afraid — otherwise the command "be not afraid" would be unnecessary. Even the great apostle experienced fear and discouragement. After Athens' modest response and Corinth's initial opposition, Paul needed divine encouragement. The Lord's promise ("I have much people in this city") sustains mission with the assurance that God has already prepared a harvest
adults.option_b: Paul was never afraid
adults.option_c: The vision was Paul's imagination
adults.option_d: Fear is incompatible with faith
adults.explanation: Acts 18:9-10 — "Be not afraid, but speak, and hold not thy peace." The command presupposes fear. Paul — stoned at Lystra, imprisoned at Philippi, chased from Thessalonica and Berea, mocked at Athens — needed reassurance. "I have much people in this city" (v.10) reveals divine foreknowledge: converts are already appointed.

adults.question: What is the significance of Gallio's ruling for the legal status of early Christianity?
adults.option_a: Gallio's dismissal of the case effectively treated Christianity as an internal Jewish matter — a religio licita (legal religion) under the umbrella of Judaism. This set an informal legal precedent: Roman officials should not prosecute Christians for religious beliefs. Gallio's ruling gave Paul (and Christianity) de facto legal protection throughout the province of Achaia
adults.option_b: Gallio was sympathetic to Christianity
adults.option_c: The ruling had no lasting impact
adults.option_d: Gallio condemned Paul
adults.explanation: Acts 18:14-16 — Gallio refused jurisdiction over an intra-Jewish religious dispute. Since Corinth was the capital of Achaia, his ruling had province-wide implications. Luke carefully records Roman officials (Gallio, Festus, Agrippa) finding Paul innocent, building a legal-apologetic case that Christianity was not a threat to Rome.

adults.question: What does the Apollos episode (18:24-28) teach about the relationship between knowledge, boldness, and teachability?
adults.option_a: Apollos was eloquent, scripturally knowledgeable, and bold — yet his understanding was incomplete ("knowing only the baptism of John"). When Priscilla and Aquila privately instructed him, he accepted correction with humility. The episode shows that spiritual gifts and rhetorical skill need theological precision, and that even gifted leaders need ongoing formation by the body of Christ
adults.option_b: Apollos was uneducated and needed basic instruction
adults.option_c: Priscilla and Aquila publicly rebuked him
adults.option_d: Apollos rejected their teaching
adults.explanation: Acts 18:24-26 — Apollos: "eloquent" (logios), "mighty in the scriptures," "instructed in the way of the Lord," "fervent in the spirit," spoke "boldly" — yet needed "the way of God more perfectly." Priscilla and Aquila's private instruction (not public correction) models gracious discipleship of gifted but incomplete leaders.

adults.question: What is the significance of Priscilla being named before Aquila in 18:26?
adults.option_a: Luke and Paul (Romans 16:3, 2 Timothy 4:19) often name Priscilla first, suggesting she was the more prominent teacher or had higher social status. Her leading role in teaching Apollos — a gifted male preacher — demonstrates that women exercised significant teaching ministry in the early church. The couple models collaborative, private theological mentoring
adults.option_b: The name order is alphabetical and meaningless
adults.option_c: Only Aquila did the teaching
adults.option_d: Luke made a scribal error
adults.explanation: Acts 18:26 — "Aquila and Priscilla" (v.2) but "Priscilla and Aquila" (v.26, also Romans 16:3, 2 Timothy 4:19). Name order reversal in ancient texts typically indicates prominence. Priscilla's leading role in instructing the eloquent Apollos is one of the clearest examples of female teaching ministry in the New Testament.

adults.question: How does Paul's declaration "from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles" (18:6) relate to his overall synagogue-first strategy?
adults.option_a: This declaration occurs in Corinth specifically (as in 13:46 for Antioch of Pisidia). Paul does not permanently abandon Jews — he returns to synagogues in later cities (18:19, 19:8). Each "turning to Gentiles" is local, not global. The pattern shows Paul fulfilling his covenantal obligation to offer the gospel to Jews first in each city, then turning to Gentiles upon rejection
adults.option_b: Paul permanently stopped preaching to Jews
adults.option_c: This was the first time Paul turned to Gentiles
adults.option_d: Paul never preached to Jews again after Corinth
adults.explanation: Acts 18:6 — "From henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles." But Paul enters the Ephesian synagogue in 18:19 and 19:8. The turning-to-Gentiles declarations (13:46, 18:6, 28:28) are local, not absolute. Paul's pattern: synagogue → rejection → Gentile mission in each city. Romans 1:16 ("to the Jew first") remains his operative principle.

adults.question: What does "I have much people in this city" (18:10) reveal about divine election and mission?
adults.option_a: God tells Paul that He already has "much people" (laos polus) in Corinth — people not yet converted but already chosen. This motivates mission: Paul's preaching will not fail because God has prepared a harvest. Divine election is the ground of missionary confidence, not a reason for passivity. Because God has chosen many, Paul should preach boldly
adults.option_b: The phrase refers to people already converted
adults.option_c: Election makes preaching unnecessary
adults.option_d: God had no people in Corinth
adults.explanation: Acts 18:10 — "I have much people (laos polus) in this city." God uses the covenant term "people" (laos, His special people) for those not yet converted. This echoes John 10:16 ("other sheep I have, which are not of this fold"). Election motivates rather than negates evangelism: preach, because God has prepared hearers.

adults.question: How does Acts 18 mark the end of the second missionary journey and what has been accomplished?
adults.option_a: Paul returns to Antioch (v.22) having established churches across Macedonia and Achaia (Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea, Athens, Corinth). The gospel has crossed from Asia to Europe, reached major cultural centres (Athens) and commercial hubs (Corinth), and gained de facto Roman legal protection (Gallio ruling). Christianity is now a trans-continental movement with a growing network of churches
adults.option_b: The second journey was a failure
adults.option_c: No new churches were established
adults.option_d: Paul returned to Jerusalem only
adults.explanation: Acts 18:22 — Paul's return to Antioch completes the second journey (15:36-18:22). Achievements: churches in Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea, Corinth; contacts in Athens; the Gallio precedent; the Priscilla/Aquila partnership; and Apollos prepared for ministry. The Gentile church now spans two continents.

kids.question: What happened when Paul laid his hands on twelve men in Ephesus?
kids.option_a: The Holy Ghost came on them and they spoke with tongues and prophesied
kids.option_b: They immediately became rich
kids.option_c: They could suddenly speak many languages
kids.option_d: They fell asleep for three days
kids.explanation: Acts 19:6 — "And when Paul had laid his hands upon them, the Holy Ghost came on them; and they spake with tongues, and prophesied."

kids.question: What did many people in Ephesus do with their magic books after believing in Jesus?
kids.option_a: They brought them together and burned them in front of everyone
kids.option_b: They gave them to the apostles
kids.option_c: They sold them at the market
kids.option_d: They hid them in a cave
kids.explanation: Acts 19:19 — "Many of them also which used curious arts brought their books together, and burned them before all men."

kids.question: Why was there a big riot in Ephesus?
kids.option_a: A silversmith named Demetrius was angry because Paul's preaching was hurting his business of making silver shrines of the goddess Diana
kids.option_b: Paul accidentally broke a statue
kids.option_c: There was a fire in the marketplace
kids.explanation: Acts 19:24-27 — "A certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, which made silver shrines for Diana, brought no small gain unto the craftsmen... this Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people, saying that they be no gods, which are made with hands."

teens.question: What did Paul discover about the twelve disciples in Ephesus?
teens.option_a: They had only received John's baptism and had not even heard whether there be any Holy Ghost — Paul baptised them in the name of the Lord Jesus
teens.option_b: They were already fully taught in the faith
teens.option_c: They were Gentiles who knew nothing about God
teens.option_d: They were Pharisees pretending to be Christians
teens.explanation: Acts 19:2-5 — "He said unto them, Have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed? And they said unto him, We have not so much as heard whether there be any Holy Ghost... When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus."

teens.question: What happened when the seven sons of Sceva tried to cast out a demon using Jesus' name?
teens.option_a: The evil spirit said "Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?" and the possessed man leaped on them, overpowered them, and they fled naked and wounded
teens.option_b: The demon left immediately
teens.option_c: Nothing happened at all
teens.option_d: They successfully cast out the demon
teens.explanation: Acts 19:15-16 — "And the evil spirit answered and said, Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye? And the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them, and overcame them, and prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded."

teens.question: How much were the magic books worth that the Ephesian believers burned?
teens.option_a: Fifty thousand pieces of silver
teens.option_b: One thousand pieces of silver
teens.option_c: Five hundred pieces of silver
teens.option_d: One hundred pieces of gold
teens.explanation: Acts 19:19 — "And they counted the price of them, and found it fifty thousand pieces of silver."

teens.question: What was Demetrius's real complaint against Paul?
teens.option_a: That Paul was destroying their profitable trade in silver shrines of Diana by convincing people that gods made with hands are not real gods
teens.option_b: That Paul owed him money
teens.option_c: That Paul was teaching people to worship the emperor
teens.option_d: That Paul had stolen silver from his shop
teens.explanation: Acts 19:26-27 — "This Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people, saying that they be no gods, which are made with hands: So that not only this our craft is in danger to be set at nought; but also that the temple of the great goddess Diana should be despised."

teens.question: How was the riot in Ephesus finally calmed down?
teens.option_a: The town clerk told the crowd they could take the matter to the proper courts and dismissed the assembly, warning that they were in danger of being charged with rioting by Rome
teens.option_b: Paul preached a sermon that calmed everyone
teens.option_c: Roman soldiers arrived and arrested the crowd
teens.option_d: A thunderstorm scattered the people
teens.explanation: Acts 19:35,40-41 — "The townclerk... appeased the people... we are in danger to be called in question for this day's uproar... And when he had thus spoken, he dismissed the assembly."

adults.question: What does the Ephesian disciples' ignorance of the Holy Spirit (19:1-7) reveal about incomplete Christianity?
adults.option_a: These disciples represent a transitional group — they had John's preparatory baptism but not the full Christian experience. Their ignorance of the Spirit shows that partial gospel knowledge exists: they believed in the coming Messiah but had not received the Spirit that marks new-covenant life. Paul's re-baptism "in the name of the Lord Jesus" and the Spirit's descent completed their initiation
adults.option_b: They were not genuine seekers
adults.option_c: John's baptism was identical to Christian baptism
adults.option_d: The Holy Spirit was not available until later
adults.explanation: Acts 19:1-7 — These disciples knew John's baptism of repentance but not Jesus' baptism of the Spirit. Paul completes their initiation: re-baptism in Jesus' name (v.5) and Spirit reception through laying on of hands (v.6). This Ephesian "mini-Pentecost" parallels the Jewish (ch.2), Samaritan (ch.8), and Gentile (ch.10) Spirit-receptions.

adults.question: What does the sons of Sceva episode teach about the difference between invoking Jesus' name and having a relationship with Him?
adults.option_a: The sons of Sceva used Jesus' name as a magical formula ("We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth") without personal faith. The demon's response — "Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?" — distinguishes between genuine authority (which comes through relationship with Christ) and counterfeit authority (which appropriates the name without the person). Spiritual power is relational, not formulaic
adults.option_b: Using Jesus' name always works regardless of faith
adults.option_c: Demons do not recognise any human authority
adults.option_d: The incident was purely symbolic
adults.explanation: Acts 19:13-16 — The demon knows Jesus (ginoskein = intimate knowledge) and Paul (epistamai = awareness), but the exorcists are unknown. Their formula — "by Jesus whom Paul preacheth" — is secondhand faith. The beating they receive demonstrates that spiritual authority cannot be borrowed, traded, or faked.

adults.question: What is the significance of the public burning of magic books worth fifty thousand pieces of silver?
adults.option_a: The burning demonstrates radical repentance — the Ephesian converts destroyed their expensive occult materials publicly, making reconciliation with sorcery impossible. Fifty thousand drachmas (equivalent to 137 years of average wages) shows the economic cost of discipleship. Luke connects this to "so mightily grew the word of God and prevailed" (v.20) — the word conquers when believers break with their past
adults.option_b: The burning was a minor event
adults.option_c: They burned the books because they were old
adults.option_d: Only a few books were destroyed
adults.explanation: Acts 19:19-20 — "Fifty thousand pieces of silver" is an enormous sum — demonstrating the depth of Ephesus's occult culture and the radicality of conversion. Public burning ensured no relapse. Luke's progress report (v.20) directly follows: the word's power is demonstrated by believers' willingness to sacrifice everything.

adults.question: How does the Demetrius riot illustrate the economic dimension of the gospel's impact?
adults.option_a: Paul's preaching that "gods made with hands are not real gods" threatened the entire idol-manufacturing economy — Demetrius frames his complaint as both economic ("our craft is in danger") and religious ("the temple of Diana despised"). The gospel inevitably disrupts economic systems built on false worship. Luke shows that the gospel has real-world economic consequences, and those who profit from idolatry resist it violently
adults.option_b: The riot was purely religious
adults.option_c: Paul was deliberately targeting businesses
adults.option_d: Economics played no role in the opposition
adults.explanation: Acts 19:25-27 — Demetrius's argument moves from economics (v.25, "our wealth") to religion (v.27, "Diana despised"). The intertwining of profit and piety is the real issue. When the gospel challenges idolatry, it necessarily challenges economies built on idolatry. The same pattern appears with the Philippian slave girl (16:19).

adults.question: What does Paul's two-year teaching ministry in the school of Tyrannus (19:9-10) reveal about his long-term strategy?
adults.option_a: Paul shifted from itinerant synagogue preaching to sustained daily teaching in a rented lecture hall for two years, resulting in "all they which dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord." Ephesus became a regional hub from which the gospel spread throughout the entire Roman province of Asia. Paul used a strategic urban centre to evangelise an entire region through trained disciples who fanned out
adults.option_b: Paul stayed because he could not find another city
adults.option_c: Two years was an unusually short stay
adults.option_d: The school was his permanent home
adults.explanation: Acts 19:9-10 — "Disputing daily in the school of one Tyrannus. And this continued by the space of two years; so that all they which dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus." Paul's Ephesus strategy: teach intensively in a central hub, and trained converts carry the gospel to surrounding cities (likely how Colossae, Laodicea, and Hierapolis were reached — Colossians 1:7, 4:13).

adults.question: What does the town clerk's speech (19:35-41) reveal about Roman civic administration and Christian apologetics?
adults.option_a: The clerk's speech is a secular defence of the Christians: he affirms that Paul and his companions are "neither robbers of churches, nor yet blasphemers of your goddess" (v.37). He channels the dispute into proper legal channels and warns of Roman intervention. Luke uses this Roman official's testimony to show that Christianity was not a public threat — part of Acts' ongoing legal apologetic
adults.option_b: The clerk was a secret Christian
adults.option_c: His speech was irrelevant to the narrative
adults.option_d: He was hostile to Paul
adults.explanation: Acts 19:37,40 — The clerk's testimony that Christians are not temple-robbers or blasphemers provides legal vindication. His warning about Rome ("we are in danger to be called in question for this day's uproar") aligns the church with civic order and paints the rioters as the real troublemakers. Luke consistently uses Roman officials to vindicate Christians.

adults.question: How does Acts 19:20 ("So mightily grew the word of God and prevailed") function as a narrative summary?
adults.option_a: This is Luke's fifth progress report (cf. 6:7, 9:31, 12:24, 16:5). Each marks the gospel's advance past a major obstacle. Here, the word prevails over occultism (the magic books), counterfeit spirituality (sons of Sceva), and economic opposition (Demetrius). The verb "prevailed" (ischuen) means to demonstrate strength — the word is a conquering force that overcomes all opposition
adults.option_b: It is a minor transitional phrase
adults.option_c: The word's growth stopped after this
adults.option_d: Only Luke uses such summaries
adults.explanation: Acts 19:20 — "So mightily grew the word of God and prevailed." The Greek katischuen means "won strength against" — military language for victory. After each obstacle (occult practices, fake exorcists, economic riot), the word grows stronger. Luke's progress reports are theological declarations: God's word cannot be stopped.

adults.question: What does the Ephesian ministry reveal about the comprehensive nature of the gospel's confrontation with culture?
adults.option_a: In Ephesus, the gospel confronts intellectual paganism (the synagogue and school of Tyrannus), popular superstition (magic books and exorcism), economic vested interests (silversmith guild), and civic religion (the Diana cult). The gospel does not leave any sphere of life unchallenged — it is not a private religious opinion but a total worldview that transforms minds, practices, economics, and public life
adults.option_b: The gospel only affected religious practices
adults.option_c: Ephesus was culturally unaffected by Paul
adults.option_d: The gospel addressed only individual salvation
adults.explanation: Acts 19 presents a comprehensive gospel impact: intellectual conversion (vv.8-10), supernatural confrontation (vv.11-16), moral transformation (vv.18-19), economic disruption (vv.24-27), and civic upheaval (vv.28-41). Ephesus is Luke's showcase of what happens when the gospel fully engages a major city.

kids.question: What happened to the young man named Eutychus who fell asleep during Paul's long sermon?
kids.option_a: He fell from the third floor window and died, but Paul went down and embraced him and said his life was in him
kids.option_b: He woke up and apologised
kids.option_c: He was carried home to sleep
kids.explanation: Acts 20:9-10 — "And there sat in a window a certain young man named Eutychus, being fallen into a deep sleep: and as Paul was long preaching, he sunk down with sleep, and fell down from the third loft, and was taken up dead. And Paul went down, and fell on him, and embracing him said, Trouble not yourselves; for his life is in him."

kids.question: Why were the Ephesian elders sad when Paul said goodbye to them?
kids.option_a: Because Paul said they would never see his face again
kids.option_b: Because Paul gave them too much work to do
kids.option_c: Because it was raining that day
kids.option_d: Because they had to walk a long distance
kids.explanation: Acts 20:38 — "Sorrowing most of all for the words which he spake, that they should see his face no more."

kids.question: What did Paul say he had not coveted from anyone?
kids.option_a: Silver, gold, or clothing — his own hands had provided for his needs and the needs of those with him
kids.option_b: Food and water
kids.option_c: Houses and land
kids.explanation: Acts 20:33-34 — "I have coveted no man's silver, or gold, or apparel. Yea, ye yourselves know, that these hands have ministered unto my necessities, and to them that were with me."

teens.question: What did Paul tell the Ephesian elders to watch out for?
teens.option_a: Grievous wolves who would come in among them, not sparing the flock — and even from among themselves men would arise, speaking perverse things to draw away disciples
teens.option_b: Roman soldiers who would destroy their church
teens.option_c: False prophets who would predict earthquakes
teens.option_d: Tax collectors who would steal their money
teens.explanation: Acts 20:29-30 — "For I know this, that after my departing shall grievous wolves enter in among you, not sparing the flock. Also of your own selves shall men arise, speaking perverse things, to draw away disciples after them."

teens.question: What did Paul say he was going to Jerusalem to face?
teens.option_a: Bonds and afflictions await me — but none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy
teens.option_b: A celebration in his honour
teens.option_c: A new position as high priest
teens.option_d: Freedom from all persecution
teens.explanation: Acts 20:23-24 — "The Holy Ghost witnesseth in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions abide me. But none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy, and the ministry, which I have received of the Lord Jesus."

teens.question: What responsibility did Paul give to the Ephesian elders?
teens.option_a: Take heed unto yourselves and to all the flock, over the which the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers, to feed the church of God
teens.option_b: Build a new temple in Ephesus
teens.option_c: Collect money for Rome
teens.option_d: Write new books of the Bible
teens.explanation: Acts 20:28 — "Take heed therefore unto yourselves, and to all the flock, over the which the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers, to feed the church of God, which he hath purchased with his own blood."

teens.question: What words of Jesus did Paul quote that are not recorded in the Gospels?
teens.option_a: It is more blessed to give than to receive
teens.option_b: Do unto others as you would have them do unto you
teens.option_c: Love your neighbour as yourself
teens.option_d: Judge not, that ye be not judged
teens.explanation: Acts 20:35 — "I have shewed you all things, how that so labouring ye ought to support the weak, and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, how he said, It is more blessed to give than to receive."

teens.question: How did Paul describe his ministry among the Ephesians?
teens.option_a: He had served the Lord with all humility, with many tears and temptations, kept back nothing profitable, and taught publicly and from house to house
teens.option_b: He had worked alone without any help
teens.option_c: He only preached on special occasions
teens.option_d: He avoided all difficulties and conflicts
teens.explanation: Acts 20:19-20 — "Serving the Lord with all humility of mind, and with many tears, and temptations... And how I kept back nothing that was profitable unto you, but have shewed you, and have taught you publickly, and from house to house."

adults.question: How does Paul's Miletus speech (20:17-38) function as his pastoral testament?
adults.option_a: This is Paul's only speech in Acts addressed to Christians (all others are to unbelievers or officials). It serves as a farewell address modelled on Moses' farewell (Deuteronomy), Samuel's farewell (1 Samuel 12), and Jesus' farewell discourse (John 13-17). It covers his past ministry (vv.18-21), his future suffering (vv.22-24), warnings about false teachers (vv.28-31), and commendation to God's grace (vv.32-35)
adults.option_b: It was a casual goodbye with no structure
adults.option_c: Paul addressed a crowd of unbelievers
adults.option_d: The speech has no Old Testament precedent
adults.explanation: Acts 20:17-38 — The farewell speech genre includes: review of past faithfulness (vv.18-21), prediction of future danger (vv.22-25,29-30), commission to the next generation (v.28), and commendation to God (v.32). Paul follows the pattern of Moses, Joshua, Samuel, and Jesus — a final charge from a departing leader.

adults.question: What does "the church of God, which he hath purchased with his own blood" (20:28) reveal theologically?
adults.option_a: This is one of the highest Christological statements in Acts: "God... purchased with his own blood" attributes the blood of the cross to God Himself. Whether read as "God's own blood" or "the blood of his Own [Son]," it connects the atonement directly to the divine nature. The church's value is measured by its purchase price — the blood of God incarnate
adults.option_b: The phrase has no Christological significance
adults.option_c: It refers to animal blood
adults.option_d: Paul was speaking metaphorically about effort
adults.explanation: Acts 20:28 — "The church of God, which he hath purchased with his own blood." The Greek (tou idiou haimatos) is striking: God's own blood purchased the church. This implies the deity of Christ (whose blood was shed) and the infinite value of the atonement. The church is precious because its purchase price was infinite.

adults.question: What does Paul's warning about "grievous wolves" (20:29-30) reveal about threats to the church?
adults.option_a: Paul identifies two threats: external ("grievous wolves" entering from outside) and internal ("of your own selves shall men arise, speaking perverse things"). The internal threat is more dangerous because it comes from trusted leaders. False teaching that draws disciples away from the truth is the church's greatest danger — worse than persecution, which typically strengthens the church
adults.option_b: Only external threats endanger the church
adults.option_c: False teachers are easily identified
adults.option_d: Paul was speaking about literal wolves
adults.explanation: Acts 20:29-30 — Two sources of danger: wolves entering from outside and men arising from within. The internal threat ("of your own selves") echoes Jesus' warning about false prophets in sheep's clothing (Matthew 7:15). Church history confirms Paul's prediction: the Ephesian church later battled internal heresy (Revelation 2:2, 1 Timothy 1:3).

adults.question: How does Paul's quotation of an unrecorded saying of Jesus ("It is more blessed to give than to receive," 20:35) affect our understanding of the Gospel traditions?
adults.option_a: This saying, not found in any canonical Gospel, demonstrates that the written Gospels do not contain everything Jesus said (cf. John 21:25). The early church preserved oral traditions of Jesus' sayings (agrapha) alongside the written accounts. Paul's access to authentic Jesus traditions through the apostolic community shows the richness of early oral tradition
adults.option_b: Paul invented this saying
adults.option_c: Only written Gospel sayings are authentic
adults.option_d: This contradicts the Gospels
adults.explanation: Acts 20:35 — "The words of the Lord Jesus, how he said, It is more blessed to give than to receive." This agraphon (unwritten saying of Jesus) confirms John 21:25 ("many other things which Jesus did"). Paul received oral tradition (cf. 1 Corinthians 11:23, 15:3) alongside what was later written in the Gospels.

adults.question: What ecclesiological terms does Paul use interchangeably in 20:17,28, and what does this imply?
adults.option_a: Paul summons "elders" (presbuteroi, v.17), tells them the Holy Ghost made them "overseers" (episkopoi, v.28), and commands them to "feed" (poimainein = shepherd) the flock. The three terms — elder, overseer, shepherd — describe the same office from different angles: elder (maturity/dignity), overseer (function/responsibility), shepherd (care/protection). This interchangeability argues against rigid hierarchical distinctions
adults.option_b: The three terms refer to three different offices
adults.option_c: Only the term "elder" has any significance
adults.option_d: Paul was confused about church structure
adults.explanation: Acts 20:17,28 — Elders (presbuteroi) are called overseers (episkopoi) who shepherd (poimainein) the flock. The same leaders bear all three designations. This parallels 1 Peter 5:1-2 ("elders... feed/shepherd the flock... taking the oversight") and Titus 1:5,7 (where elder and overseer are used for the same person).

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "I am pure from the blood of all men" (20:26)?
adults.option_a: Paul echoes Ezekiel 33:1-9's watchman metaphor — if the watchman warns and the people do not listen, their blood is on their own heads; but if he fails to warn, their blood is on him. Paul's faithful proclamation of "all the counsel of God" (v.27) means he bears no responsibility for those who reject the gospel. He has discharged his duty as Christ's watchman
adults.option_b: Paul was saying he never hurt anyone physically
adults.option_c: He was boasting about his moral perfection
adults.option_d: The phrase is a Roman legal defence
adults.explanation: Acts 20:26-27 — "I am pure from the blood of all men. For I have not shunned to declare unto you all the counsel of God." The Ezekiel 33 watchman principle: the herald who faithfully declares the message is freed from accountability for the hearers' response. Paul's conscience is clear because he withheld nothing.

adults.question: How does the Eutychus incident (20:7-12) provide insights into early Christian worship?
