adults.option_a: The passage reveals several worship practices: meeting on "the first day of the week" (Sunday, v.7), "breaking of bread" (likely Lord's Supper, v.7), and extended teaching ("Paul preached unto them... continued his speech until midnight," v.7). The setting — an upper room with many lamps (v.8), an extended gathering — shows early Christians worshipping in homes with both sacramental and teaching elements
adults.option_b: Early Christians only met on Saturday
adults.option_c: The incident tells us nothing about worship
adults.option_d: Breaking of bread was an ordinary meal
adults.explanation: Acts 20:7-8 — "Upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them... there were many lights in the upper chamber." Sunday gathering, Lord's Supper, extended teaching, domestic setting, evening meeting — these details provide a window into first-century house-church worship.

adults.question: What does Paul's phrase "I count not my life dear unto myself" (20:24) reveal about apostolic motivation?
adults.option_a: Paul subordinates self-preservation to mission completion — his life's value is measured not by its length but by its faithfulness to the calling. "Finish my course with joy" echoes an athletic metaphor (cf. 2 Timothy 4:7). The apostle's motivation is not survival but obedience. This transforms suffering from obstacle to opportunity and fear into freedom
adults.option_b: Paul had a death wish
adults.option_c: He was indifferent to his own wellbeing
adults.option_d: This was rhetorical exaggeration
adults.explanation: Acts 20:24 — "Neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy, and the ministry." Paul's motivation: course completion, not self-preservation. The athletic metaphor (dromos = race) appears in 2 Timothy 4:7 ("I have finished my course"). Freedom from the fear of death liberates for radical obedience.

kids.question: What did the prophet Agabus do with Paul's belt to warn him?
kids.option_a: He tied his own hands and feet with it and said the Jews in Jerusalem would bind Paul and deliver him to the Gentiles
kids.option_b: He threw it into the river
kids.option_c: He stretched it between two trees
kids.option_d: He tied it around his own waist
kids.explanation: Acts 21:11 — "And when he was come unto us, he took Paul's girdle, and bound his own hands and feet, and said, Thus saith the Holy Ghost, So shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man that owneth this girdle, and shall deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles."

kids.question: What did Paul say when his friends begged him not to go to Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: I am ready not to be bound only, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus
kids.option_b: I am too afraid to go
kids.option_c: Maybe I will change my plans
kids.option_d: You are right, I should stay here
kids.explanation: Acts 21:13 — "Then Paul answered, What mean ye to weep and to break mine heart? for I am ready not to be bound only, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus."

kids.question: What happened to Paul in the temple at Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: Jews from Asia stirred up the crowd, grabbed him, and dragged him out of the temple, trying to kill him
kids.option_b: He was welcomed with a big celebration
kids.option_c: He taught a peaceful sermon
kids.option_d: The priests gave him a gift
kids.explanation: Acts 21:27,30 — "The Jews which were of Asia, when they saw him in the temple, stirred up all the people, and laid hands on him... and they took Paul, and drew him out of the temple."

teens.question: What did the prophet Agabus predict would happen to Paul?
teens.option_a: The Jews in Jerusalem would bind Paul and deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles — he acted it out by binding his own hands and feet with Paul's belt
teens.option_b: Paul would become the ruler of Jerusalem
teens.option_c: A great earthquake would destroy the temple
teens.option_d: Paul would be set free from all his enemies
teens.explanation: Acts 21:11 — "He took Paul's girdle, and bound his own hands and feet, and said, Thus saith the Holy Ghost, So shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man that owneth this girdle, and shall deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles."

teens.question: What did James and the elders suggest Paul do to show he still respected the law?
teens.option_a: Take four men who had a vow, go through the purification rites with them, and pay their expenses — to show everyone that Paul himself walked orderly and kept the law
teens.option_b: Offer a sacrifice of one hundred lambs
teens.option_c: Preach a sermon defending the law of Moses
teens.option_d: Write a letter to all the synagogues
teens.explanation: Acts 21:23-24 — "Take them, and purify thyself with them, and be at charges with them, that they may shave their heads: and all may know that those things, whereof they were informed concerning thee, are nothing; but that thou thyself also walkest orderly, and keepest the law."

teens.question: Why did the Asian Jews start the riot against Paul in the temple?
teens.option_a: They falsely accused Paul of bringing a Gentile (Trophimus the Ephesian) into the temple — they had seen Paul with him in the city and assumed he brought him into the temple
teens.option_b: Paul had broken a temple lamp
teens.option_c: Paul was wearing Gentile clothing
teens.option_d: Paul had challenged the high priest publicly
teens.explanation: Acts 21:28-29 — "This is the man, that teacheth all men every where against the people, and the law, and this place: and further brought Greeks also into the temple, and hath polluted this holy place. (For they had seen before with him in the city Trophimus an Ephesian, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple.)"

teens.question: Who rescued Paul from the mob that was trying to kill him?
teens.option_a: The Roman chief captain and his soldiers, who ran down and arrested Paul, binding him with two chains
teens.option_b: The temple priests stopped the crowd
teens.option_c: Paul's friends fought off the attackers
teens.option_d: An angel appeared and frightened the mob
teens.explanation: Acts 21:31-33 — "And as they went about to kill him, tidings came unto the chief captain of the band, that all Jerusalem was in an uproar. Who immediately took soldiers and centurions, and ran down unto them... and commanded him to be bound with two chains."

teens.question: What did Paul ask the chief captain for permission to do?
teens.option_a: To speak to the people from the stairs of the barracks — and he addressed the crowd in the Hebrew tongue
teens.option_b: To be released from his chains
teens.option_c: To send a letter to Rome
teens.option_d: To return to the temple to pray
teens.explanation: Acts 21:39-40 — "I beseech thee, suffer me to speak unto the people... he stood on the stairs, and beckoned with the hand unto the people. And when there was made a great silence, he spake unto them in the Hebrew tongue."

adults.question: How does Agabus's prophecy (21:10-14) relate to Paul's determination to go to Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: Agabus prophesies accurately (Paul will be bound and delivered to Gentiles) but does not command Paul to avoid Jerusalem. The friends' plea ("we besought him not to go up") is their interpretation of the prophecy, not its content. Paul discerns that the Spirit reveals what will happen without forbidding it — knowing the cost does not change the calling. "The will of the Lord be done" (v.14) shows communal submission
adults.option_b: Agabus forbade Paul to go to Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Paul disobeyed the Holy Spirit by going
adults.option_d: The prophecy was inaccurate
adults.explanation: Acts 21:11-14 — Agabus says "Thus saith the Holy Ghost" but prophesies events, not commands. Compare 20:23 where the Spirit "witnesseth" bonds and afflictions without saying "don't go." Paul distinguishes between prophetic revelation (this will happen) and divine command (do/don't do this). The community submits: "The will of the Lord be done."

adults.question: What does the James compromise (21:23-26) reveal about Paul's flexibility and the Jerusalem church's concerns?
adults.option_a: Paul agrees to undergo a Nazirite purification to demonstrate that he does not oppose Jewish practice for Jewish believers — he opposes only imposing it on Gentiles. This is consistent with 1 Corinthians 9:20 ("unto the Jews I became as a Jew"). James's concern is pastoral: thousands of Jewish believers are "zealous of the law" (v.20) and need reassurance. Paul accommodates for unity, not compromise
adults.option_b: Paul was being hypocritical
adults.option_c: James forced Paul to abandon his theology
adults.option_d: Paul secretly opposed the law
adults.explanation: Acts 21:20-24 — James reports "how many thousands of Jews there are which believe; and they are all zealous of the law." The purification rite was not a betrayal of Paul's gospel but an application of his "all things to all men" principle (1 Corinthians 9:19-23). Jewish Christians could observe the law as long as it was not made a requirement for salvation.

adults.question: How does the false charge against Paul (bringing Gentiles into the temple) echo a larger thematic issue in Acts?
adults.option_a: The charge — that Paul "brought Greeks into the temple" (v.28) — is precisely what Paul's gospel does spiritually: bringing Gentiles into God's presence without the temple barrier. The physical accusation is false (Paul did not bring Trophimus in), but the spiritual reality is true: Christ broke down the "middle wall of partition" (Ephesians 2:14). The charge inadvertently captures the gospel's revolutionary nature
adults.option_b: The charge was completely irrelevant
adults.option_c: Paul actually did bring Trophimus in
adults.option_d: The barrier between Jew and Gentile was never discussed in Acts
adults.explanation: Acts 21:28-29 — The temple's Court of the Gentiles was separated by a wall with death-penalty inscriptions for Gentile trespassers (archaeological fragments survive). Paul's gospel declares this wall abolished in Christ (Ephesians 2:14). The false physical charge ironically states the spiritual truth Paul proclaimed.

adults.question: What does Paul's arrest in the temple mark in the narrative structure of Acts?
adults.option_a: Paul's arrest begins his passion narrative — paralleling Jesus' journey to Jerusalem, arrest, trials before Jewish and Roman authorities, and journey to Rome (=death). From chapter 21 to 28, Paul is a prisoner. Like Jesus, he enters Jerusalem knowing what awaits (cf. Luke 9:51, Acts 20:22-24), is seized in the temple, faces the Sanhedrin, is delivered to Roman custody, and is repeatedly declared innocent
adults.option_b: The arrest is a minor incident
adults.option_c: Paul's story ends here
adults.option_d: There are no parallels with Jesus' passion
adults.explanation: Acts 21:33 — Paul bound with two chains (cf. Jesus bound, Mark 15:1). The parallels with Jesus' passion are extensive: Jerusalem journey with foreknowledge (20:22/Luke 9:51), temple arrest (21:30/Luke 22:54), Sanhedrin trial (22:30-23:10/Luke 22:66-71), Roman trial (24-26/Luke 23:1-25), innocence declared (23:29, 25:25, 26:31/Luke 23:4,14,22). Paul walks in his Master's footsteps.

adults.question: What does Luke's mention of Philip the evangelist and his four prophesying daughters (21:8-9) contribute to the narrative?
adults.option_a: Luke connects the current narrative to the earlier Jerusalem church (Philip from Acts 6-8), showing continuity. The four daughters who prophesy fulfil Joel 2:28-29 (quoted in Acts 2:17-18): "your sons and your daughters shall prophesy." Their mention validates women's prophetic ministry and shows the Pentecostal promise being realised in the second generation of the church
adults.option_b: Philip's daughters are irrelevant detail
adults.option_c: Only sons could prophesy
adults.option_d: Philip was a different person from Acts 6
adults.explanation: Acts 21:8-9 — "Philip the evangelist, which was one of the seven... the same man had four daughters, virgins, which did prophesy." Philip connects Acts 6 (the seven) with Acts 21. His prophesying daughters fulfil Acts 2:17-18 (Joel 2:28-29). Women prophesying demonstrates the Spirit's gender-inclusive gifting.

adults.question: How does the Roman military's intervention (21:31-36) serve God's purposes?
adults.option_a: The Roman soldiers unwittingly save Paul's life — the mob was "about to kill him" (v.31) when the chief captain intervened. Throughout Acts, Roman power inadvertently serves the gospel: protecting Paul from the mob, providing legal process, and eventually transporting him to Rome (fulfilling 23:11). God uses secular authority as an instrument of His sovereign plan
adults.option_b: The Romans were trying to help Paul evangelise
adults.option_c: Roman intervention was harmful to Paul
adults.option_d: God only works through the church, not through secular powers
adults.explanation: Acts 21:31-32 — "As they went about to kill him, tidings came unto the chief captain... Who immediately took soldiers and centurions, and ran down unto them." The Roman army saves the church's greatest missionary. This pattern (secular power serving divine purposes) echoes Joseph in Egypt, Cyrus in Isaiah, and Pilate at the cross.

adults.question: What does Paul's request to address the crowd in Hebrew (21:39-40) reveal about his rhetorical strategy?
adults.option_a: Paul identifies himself as a Tarsus citizen (credibility with the captain) and then speaks in Hebrew/Aramaic (connection with the Jewish crowd). By switching to the vernacular, he silences the mob ("when they heard that he spake in the Hebrew tongue, they kept the more silence," 22:2). Paul adapts his language to his audience — a consistent principle of his missionary method
adults.option_b: Paul only spoke Hebrew
adults.option_c: Speaking Hebrew was unusual for Paul
adults.option_d: The crowd could not understand Hebrew
adults.explanation: Acts 21:40, 22:2 — Paul's strategic bilingualism: Greek to the Roman captain (21:37), Hebrew/Aramaic to the Jewish crowd (21:40). The crowd's increased silence at hearing their own language demonstrates the power of cultural identification. Paul embodies his own principle: "I am made all things to all men" (1 Corinthians 9:22).

adults.question: How does Acts 21 demonstrate the theme of divine sovereignty working through human opposition?
adults.option_a: Every hostile act advances God's plan: the riot leads to Roman custody, which leads to testimony before governors and kings (as Jesus predicted in Luke 21:12-13), which leads to Rome. The accusers intended to destroy Paul; God intended to transport him. The same pattern governs all of Acts: persecution scatters the church (8:1), opposition creates legal precedents (18:14-16), and imprisonment enables witness (28:30-31)
adults.option_b: God was absent during Paul's arrest
adults.option_c: The arrest was a setback for the mission
adults.option_d: Human opposition always defeats God's purposes
adults.explanation: Acts 21-28 — Paul's arrest (21:33) leads to witness before the Sanhedrin (23:1), Felix (24:10), Festus (25:8), Agrippa (26:1), and ultimately Rome (28:16). Jesus had said: "Thou must bear witness also at Rome" (23:11). Every step of opposition becomes a step toward the mission's completion.

kids.question: What did Paul tell the crowd about his life before he met Jesus?
kids.option_a: He was brought up in Jerusalem, taught by Gamaliel according to the law, and was very zealous for God — he persecuted Christians
kids.option_b: He was a fisherman from Galilee
kids.option_c: He was a Roman soldier
kids.option_d: He was a farmer in Egypt
kids.explanation: Acts 22:3 — "I am verily a man which am a Jew, born in Tarsus, a city in Cilicia, yet brought up in this city at the feet of Gamaliel, and taught according to the perfect manner of the law of the fathers, and was zealous toward God."

kids.question: What made the crowd stop listening and start shouting again?
kids.option_a: When Paul said that Jesus had sent him to preach to the Gentiles
kids.option_b: When Paul said something bad about Moses
kids.option_c: When Paul refused to speak in Hebrew
kids.option_d: When Paul asked them for money
kids.explanation: Acts 22:21-22 — "And he said unto me, Depart: for I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles. And they gave him audience unto this word, and then lifted up their voices, and said, Away with such a fellow from the earth: for it is not fit that he should live."

kids.question: What stopped the Roman soldiers from whipping Paul?
kids.option_a: Paul told them he was a Roman citizen, and it was against the law to beat a Roman without a trial
kids.option_b: Paul ran away from the soldiers
kids.option_c: An angel appeared and stopped them
kids.option_d: The crowd demanded they let Paul go
kids.explanation: Acts 22:25-26 — "And as they bound him with thongs, Paul said unto the centurion that stood by, Is it lawful for you to scourge a man that is a Roman, and uncondemned? When the centurion heard that, he went and told the chief captain."

teens.question: How did Paul describe his encounter with Jesus on the road to Damascus?
teens.option_a: A great light from heaven shone around him at noon, he fell to the ground, and heard a voice saying "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" — and the voice identified himself as Jesus of Nazareth
teens.option_b: He saw a burning bush like Moses
teens.option_c: An angel appeared with a written message
teens.option_d: He heard a voice in the temple
teens.explanation: Acts 22:6-8 — "And it came to pass, that, as I made my journey, and was come nigh unto Damascus about noon, suddenly there shone from heaven a great light round about me. And I fell unto the ground, and heard a voice saying unto me, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? And I answered, Who art thou, Lord? And he said unto me, I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest."

teens.question: What was the word that triggered the crowd's violent reaction?
teens.option_a: The word "Gentiles" — when Paul said the Lord sent him to preach to the Gentiles, the crowd erupted in rage
teens.option_b: The word "Caesar"
teens.option_c: The word "temple"
teens.option_d: The word "Moses"
teens.explanation: Acts 22:21-22 — "I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles. And they gave him audience unto this word, and then lifted up their voices." The idea that God would include Gentiles on equal terms was intolerable to the crowd.

teens.question: What did Ananias say to Paul about God's purpose for him?
teens.option_a: The God of our fathers hath chosen thee, that thou shouldest know his will, and see that Just One, and hear the voice of his mouth — and thou shalt be his witness unto all men
teens.option_b: You should return to being a Pharisee
teens.option_c: God wants you to stay in Damascus forever
teens.option_d: You should become the high priest
teens.explanation: Acts 22:14-15 — "The God of our fathers hath chosen thee, that thou shouldest know his will, and see that Just One, and shouldest hear the voice of his mouth. For thou shalt be his witness unto all men of what thou hast seen and heard."

teens.question: How did the chief captain react when he learned Paul was a Roman citizen?
teens.option_a: He was afraid because he had bound Paul without a trial, and he himself had bought his citizenship at a great price, while Paul was free-born
teens.option_b: He did not care about Paul's citizenship
teens.option_c: He released Paul immediately
teens.option_d: He called for Paul to be executed
teens.explanation: Acts 22:28-29 — "And the chief captain answered, With a great sum obtained I this freedom. And Paul said, But I was free born. Then straightway they departed from him which should have examined him: and the chief captain also was afraid, after he knew that he was a Roman."

teens.question: What did the chief captain do next to find out what Paul had done?
teens.option_a: He commanded the chief priests and the Sanhedrin to assemble so he could bring Paul before them
teens.option_b: He sent Paul to Rome immediately
teens.option_c: He released Paul and closed the case
teens.option_d: He asked the emperor for instructions
teens.explanation: Acts 22:30 — "On the morrow, because he would have known the certainty wherefore he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him from his bands, and commanded the chief priests and all their council to appear, and brought Paul down, and set him before them."

adults.question: How does Paul's second telling of his conversion (Acts 22) adapt the story for a Jewish audience?
adults.option_a: Paul emphasises his impeccable Jewish credentials: born in Tarsus but raised in Jerusalem, trained under Gamaliel, zealous for the law (v.3). He highlights Ananias as "a devout man according to the law, having a good report of all the Jews" (v.12) and frames his commission in terms of the God of their fathers (v.14). He delays the explosive word "Gentiles" until the end — showing rhetorical sensitivity to his audience
adults.option_b: Paul told the same story identically every time
adults.option_c: He omitted his Jewish background
adults.option_d: He emphasised his Gentile connections
adults.explanation: Acts 22:3,12,14 — Paul tailors his testimony: Jewish pedigree (Gamaliel), Ananias's Jewish credentials, the "God of our fathers" formula. He builds maximum common ground before the divisive climax: "I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles" (v.21). Contextualisation is not compromise — it is wisdom.

adults.question: Why did the mention of "Gentiles" provoke such violent rage from the crowd?
adults.option_a: The crowd tolerated Paul's testimony about a heavenly vision and even a commission to witness — but the claim that Israel's God would send a Jewish apostle specifically to Gentiles crossed an ideological boundary. This implied Gentile equality before God, which threatened Jewish ethnic privilege and national identity. The rage reveals that exclusivism, not theology, was the real issue
adults.option_b: The crowd was angry about a different word
adults.option_c: They objected to Paul's vision, not his mission
adults.option_d: Gentile inclusion was universally accepted
adults.explanation: Acts 22:21-22 — "Gentiles" triggered the explosion. The crowd could tolerate Paul's Damascus road story but not its implication: God's salvation for Gentiles on equal terms. This mirrors Nazareth's rage when Jesus cited God's blessings on Gentiles (Luke 4:24-29). Jewish exclusivism could not tolerate a universal gospel.

adults.question: What does Paul's Roman citizenship reveal about his social position and how he uses it strategically?
adults.option_a: Paul was born a Roman citizen (civis Romanus natus) — a privilege his family acquired, suggesting either notable service or wealth. He uses it strategically: not to avoid suffering (he had already been beaten) but to prevent extrajudicial punishment that could discredit the gospel. At Philippi (16:37), he used it to protect the church; here he uses it to ensure proper legal process
adults.option_b: Paul invented his citizenship claim
adults.option_c: Roman citizenship was common and unimportant
adults.option_d: Paul always used citizenship to avoid suffering
adults.explanation: Acts 22:25-28 — The centurion "bought" his citizenship (v.28), but Paul was "free born" — born into it, suggesting a family of some status. Paul deploys citizenship selectively: he suffered without invoking it many times (2 Corinthians 11:25). Here, scourging would have been extrajudicial torture (examination by flogging) — Paul prevents this abuse of Roman law.

adults.question: What does Paul's temple vision (22:17-21) add to the conversion accounts that chapters 9 and 26 omit?
adults.option_a: This account uniquely includes a post-conversion temple vision where Jesus directly tells Paul to leave Jerusalem because they "will not receive thy testimony" and specifically commissions him: "I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles." This vision, only recorded here, connects Paul's Gentile mission directly to a dominical command received in the temple itself — God sent Paul from the holiest Jewish space to the Gentile world
adults.option_b: The temple vision appears in all three accounts
adults.option_c: It contradicts the other conversion narratives
adults.option_d: Paul invented the vision for rhetorical purposes
adults.explanation: Acts 22:17-21 — This temple trance is unique to the Acts 22 account. It accomplishes two things for the Jewish audience: (1) Paul was praying in the temple (not opposing it), and (2) his Gentile mission was a direct divine command, not personal choice. The irony: the temple itself sent Paul to the Gentiles.

adults.question: How does the chief captain's purchased versus Paul's inherited citizenship illustrate Roman social dynamics?
adults.option_a: Claudius Lysias "bought" (ektesamen) citizenship at "great price" — common under Emperor Claudius when freedmen sold citizenship for profit. Paul's freeborn status (eleutheros) was higher, acquired through family (possibly ancestors received it for service to Rome). The contrast reveals social stratification: purchased citizens were common but freeborn citizens had greater prestige. Paul's status gives him legal superiority over his own captor
adults.option_b: Both types of citizenship were identical
adults.option_c: Purchased citizenship was more prestigious
adults.option_d: Paul's citizenship was unverifiable
adults.explanation: Acts 22:28 — "With a great sum obtained I this freedom. But I was free born." Under Claudius, citizenship sales were widespread (Dio Cassius 60.17). Birth citizenship (from a citizen father) was more prestigious. The irony: the Roman commander is socially outranked by his prisoner — Roman law protects Paul against Roman abuse.

adults.question: What does Paul's appeal to his persecuting past (22:4-5,19-20) serve in his rhetorical argument?
adults.option_a: By emphasising his former zeal against Christians — "I persecuted this way unto the death" (v.4), "I imprisoned and beat them" (v.19), "I kept the raiment of them that slew Stephen" (v.20) — Paul establishes two things: he understands his audience's hostility (he once shared it), and only divine intervention could have changed him. The dramatic reversal makes his testimony compelling — he was the least likely convert
adults.option_b: Paul was boasting about his past
adults.option_c: His persecution history was irrelevant
adults.option_d: Paul was trying to gain sympathy from the Romans
adults.explanation: Acts 22:4-5,19-20 — Paul's pre-conversion biography mirrors his audience's current hostility: "I persecuted this way unto the death." His point: I was exactly like you — zealous, violent, convinced I was right. Only a divine intervention of overwhelming power could have turned the church's greatest enemy into its greatest advocate.

adults.question: How does Luke use the chief captain's investigation to advance his apologetic purposes?
adults.option_a: Claudius Lysias represents Roman justice seeking the truth. His process — rescuing Paul, investigating the charges, arranging a hearing before the Sanhedrin — shows Roman administration functioning properly. Luke consistently portrays Roman officials as procedurally fair (Gallio, Lysias, Felix, Festus, Agrippa), reinforcing the argument that Christianity is not a criminal movement
adults.option_b: Luke was criticising Roman administration
adults.option_c: The chief captain had no interest in justice
adults.option_d: Roman procedure was irrelevant to Luke's purposes
adults.explanation: Acts 22:24,30 — Lysias investigates fairly: he rescues Paul (21:32), attempts examination (22:24), stops when he learns of citizenship (22:29), and convenes the Sanhedrin for a proper hearing (22:30). Luke's apologetic: Roman officials who investigate fairly find Christianity innocent. The system works; only Jewish hostility creates the problem.

adults.question: What does Ananias's description as "a devout man according to the law, having a good report of all the Jews" (22:12) accomplish rhetorically?
adults.option_a: By presenting Ananias as an observant Jew respected by the Jewish community — not a law-breaking renegade — Paul undermines the narrative that Christians are enemies of Judaism. Ananias represents the many Jewish believers who faithfully observe the law. Paul's messenger was an impeccably Jewish disciple, showing that the gospel is not anti-Jewish but the fulfilment of Jewish faith
adults.option_b: Ananias was actually a Gentile
adults.option_c: His Jewish credentials were unimportant
adults.option_d: Paul was exaggerating about Ananias
adults.explanation: Acts 22:12 — "And one Ananias, a devout man according to the law, having a good report of all the Jews which dwelt there." In Acts 9, Ananias is simply "a disciple." Here Paul highlights his Jewish piety for his Jewish audience — showing that faithful Jews can embrace Jesus without abandoning the law. The gospel is not apostasy from Judaism.

kids.question: What did Paul cleverly say to divide the Sanhedrin?
kids.option_a: I am a Pharisee and I am being judged for believing in the resurrection of the dead — this caused the Pharisees and Sadducees to argue with each other
kids.option_b: He told them a riddle they could not answer
kids.option_c: He challenged them to a contest
kids.option_d: He asked them to vote on his case
kids.explanation: Acts 23:6-7 — "But when Paul perceived that the one part were Sadducees, and the other Pharisees, he cried out in the council, Men and brethren, I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee: of the hope and resurrection of the dead I am called in question. And when he had so said, there arose a dissension between the Pharisees and the Sadducees."

kids.question: What encouraging message did the Lord give Paul at night?
kids.option_a: Be of good cheer, Paul: for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou bear witness also at Rome
kids.option_b: Go back to Tarsus and rest
kids.option_c: Build a church in this city
kids.option_d: Everything will be easy from now on
kids.explanation: Acts 23:11 — "And the night following the Lord stood by him, and said, Be of good cheer, Paul: for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou bear witness also at Rome."

kids.question: Who warned the Roman soldiers about a secret plot to kill Paul?
kids.option_a: Paul's nephew — his sister's son heard about the plot and told the chief captain
kids.option_b: An angel appeared to the captain
kids.option_c: A Pharisee wrote a secret letter
kids.option_d: Paul had a dream about it
kids.explanation: Acts 23:16 — "And when Paul's sister's son heard of their lying in wait, he went and entered into the castle, and told Paul."

teens.question: What was the difference between the Pharisees and Sadducees that Paul used to his advantage?
teens.option_a: The Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit, but the Pharisees confess all three — Paul declared himself a Pharisee judged for believing in the resurrection, splitting the council
teens.option_b: The Sadducees wore white and the Pharisees wore black
teens.option_c: The Pharisees were richer than the Sadducees
teens.option_d: The Sadducees were from Galilee and the Pharisees from Judaea
teens.explanation: Acts 23:8 — "For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, neither angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confess both."

teens.question: How many men made an oath not to eat or drink until they had killed Paul?
teens.option_a: More than forty men
teens.option_b: Twelve men
teens.option_c: Seven men
teens.option_d: One hundred men
teens.explanation: Acts 23:13 — "And they were more than forty which had made this conspiracy."

teens.question: What did the chief captain do after learning of the plot to kill Paul?
teens.option_a: He sent Paul to Governor Felix in Caesarea with a guard of two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen — by night
teens.option_b: He moved Paul to a different prison cell
teens.option_c: He dismissed the Jewish leaders
teens.option_d: He ignored the threat as insignificant
teens.explanation: Acts 23:23-24 — "He called unto him two centurions, saying, Make ready two hundred soldiers to go to Caesarea, and horsemen threescore and ten, and spearmen two hundred, at the third hour of the night; And provide them beasts, that they may set Paul on, and bring him safe unto Felix the governor."

teens.question: What did the chief captain write in his letter to Governor Felix about Paul?
teens.option_a: That Paul was accused by the Jews concerning questions of their law but had committed no crime worthy of death or imprisonment
teens.option_b: That Paul was a dangerous criminal who should be executed
teens.option_c: That Paul was a Roman spy
teens.option_d: That Paul should be sent back to Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Acts 23:29 — "Whom I perceived to be accused of questions of their law, but to have nothing laid to his charge worthy of death or of bonds."

teens.question: What happened when the high priest Ananias commanded someone to strike Paul on the mouth?
teens.option_a: Paul said "God shall smite thee, thou whited wall" — and when told he had spoken against the high priest, Paul said he did not know it was the high priest
teens.option_b: Paul turned the other cheek silently
teens.option_c: Paul struck back immediately
teens.option_d: Paul asked the guards to protect him
teens.explanation: Acts 23:2-5 — "The high priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him to smite him on the mouth. Then said Paul unto him, God shall smite thee, thou whited wall... Then said Paul, I wist not, brethren, that he was the high priest: for it is written, Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people."

adults.question: What does the Lord's night vision to Paul (23:11) accomplish narratively and theologically?
adults.option_a: The vision confirms three things: encouragement ("be of good cheer"), validation of Paul's Jerusalem testimony, and the divine promise of Rome ("so must thou bear witness also at Rome"). The word "must" (dei) makes the Roman mission a divine necessity. From this point, every obstacle (assassination plot, imprisonment, shipwreck) is reframed: nothing can prevent what God has decreed
adults.option_b: The vision was Paul's imagination
adults.option_c: It only affected Paul personally
adults.option_d: The vision was conditional on Paul's obedience
adults.explanation: Acts 23:11 — "Be of good cheer, Paul: for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou bear witness also at Rome." The divine "must" (dei) makes Paul's arrival in Rome as certain as prophecy. Every subsequent chapter confirms this: the assassination plot fails, Felix keeps Paul but cannot harm him, Festus sends him to Caesar, the shipwreck does not kill him.

adults.question: Was Paul's exploitation of the Pharisee-Sadducee division (23:6-8) legitimate strategy or dishonest manipulation?
adults.option_a: Paul's claim to be "a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee" was factually true, and his statement about resurrection was the genuine theological issue. The resurrection was both the doctrinal point of contention and the heart of the gospel. Paul framed the trial correctly: the real issue was whether Jesus rose from the dead. His strategy was truthful even if tactically shrewd
adults.option_b: Paul was lying about being a Pharisee
adults.option_c: The resurrection was irrelevant to the case
adults.option_d: Paul was simply being manipulative
adults.explanation: Acts 23:6 — Paul's double claim is factual: he was a Pharisee by training (Philippians 3:5) and the resurrection was indeed the core issue (cf. 24:15,21; 26:6-8). By identifying the theological centre, Paul exposed the council's internal contradictions and prevented a unified condemnation. Truth and strategy aligned.

adults.question: What does Paul's response to the high priest ("God shall smite thee, thou whited wall") echo, and how does Paul handle the aftermath?
adults.option_a: Paul's "whited wall" echoes Jesus' "whited sepulchres" (Matthew 23:27) — both describe hypocrisy that appears righteous outside but is corrupt within. When informed he addressed the high priest, Paul immediately quotes Exodus 22:28 ("Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people"), showing respect for the office despite condemning the man's injustice. His retraction shows submission to Scripture
adults.option_b: Paul was simply rude with no deeper meaning
adults.option_c: He deliberately insulted the high priest
adults.option_d: Paul never corrected himself
adults.explanation: Acts 23:3,5 — "Whited wall" parallels Matthew 23:27. Paul's correction ("I wist not that he was the high priest") may be literal (poor eyesight, unfamiliar robes) or ironic (this man acts nothing like a high priest). Either way, Paul defers to Scripture (Exodus 22:28), modelling principle over emotion.

adults.question: How does the assassination conspiracy (23:12-22) and its failure illustrate divine providence?
adults.option_a: Forty men take an oath to kill Paul, devising an elaborate ambush plan. Yet God thwarts the conspiracy through an ordinary means — Paul's nephew overhearing the plot. The providential chain: a boy hears, tells Paul, Paul sends him to the captain, and an army of 470 soldiers escorts Paul to safety. God's sovereignty operates through human intelligence-gathering and military logistics
adults.option_b: The conspiracy was never a real threat
adults.option_c: Paul escaped through his own cleverness
adults.option_d: God used supernatural means to save Paul
adults.explanation: Acts 23:12-24 — The plot: 40+ men, an oath, a trap. The counter: one nephew, one message, one captain, 470 soldiers. God's protection works through ordinary means: family connections, military protocols, and administrative transfers. No miracle occurs — yet the outcome is as certain as if an angel appeared, because God promised Rome (v.11).

adults.question: What does Claudius Lysias's letter to Felix (23:25-30) reveal about how Roman officials reported on Paul?
adults.option_a: The letter sanitises Lysias's own conduct (he omits binding Paul illegally and ordering the flogging) while accurately reporting that Paul committed no crime worthy of death. Despite the self-serving editing, the letter's essential conclusion serves Luke's apologetic: a Roman official finds no criminal charge against Paul. Luke includes the letter to show that secular authorities exonerated Christianity
adults.option_b: The letter was completely accurate
adults.option_c: Lysias condemned Paul in the letter
adults.option_d: The letter was invented by Luke
adults.explanation: Acts 23:27-29 — Lysias claims he rescued Paul because "I understood that he was a Roman" — actually, he learned this only after arresting and nearly flogging him. The self-serving revision aside, the critical verdict stands: "nothing laid to his charge worthy of death or of bonds" (v.29). Luke's interest is the exoneration.

adults.question: How does Paul's transfer from Jerusalem to Caesarea advance both the plot and the theological theme of Acts?
adults.option_a: The transfer removes Paul from the volatile Jerusalem environment to the Roman administrative capital of Judaea, placing him under direct Roman jurisdiction. This sets up the extended trial narratives (chapters 24-26) before Felix, Festus, and Agrippa, fulfilling both 23:11 (Rome) and Jesus' prediction that disciples would testify before governors and kings (Luke 21:12-13)
adults.option_b: The transfer was a routine administrative matter
adults.option_c: Paul preferred Caesarea to Jerusalem
adults.option_d: The transfer delayed the gospel
adults.explanation: Acts 23:23-33 — Jerusalem to Caesarea: from Jewish jurisdiction to Roman. This geographic shift mirrors Acts' overall movement from Jewish centre to Roman capital. Paul will testify before Felix (governor), Festus (governor), and Agrippa (king) — fulfilling Luke 21:12-13 and Acts 9:15 ("to bear my name before Gentiles, and kings").

adults.question: What does the Pharisees' defence of Paul (23:9 — "if a spirit or an angel hath spoken to him, let us not fight against God") recall from earlier in Acts?
adults.option_a: This echoes Gamaliel's counsel in Acts 5:38-39 ("if it be of God, ye cannot overthrow it; lest haply ye be found even to fight against God"). The Pharisees use the same theological logic: if Paul's experience is divine, opposing him means fighting God. Luke shows that even Paul's enemies sometimes speak truth, and the Pharisaic caution about opposing God proves wiser than the Sadducean rejection
adults.option_b: The Pharisees had never defended a Christian before
adults.option_c: This has no connection to Gamaliel's speech
adults.option_d: The Pharisees were all secret Christians
adults.explanation: Acts 23:9 — "If a spirit or an angel hath spoken to him, let us not fight against God." This directly echoes Gamaliel (5:39). The Pharisees' openness to resurrection, angels, and spirits (v.8) makes them natural, if temporary, allies. Luke shows the Pharisees unwittingly vindicating the gospel.

adults.question: What does the massive military escort (470 soldiers) reveal about the perceived threat level and Roman administrative priorities?
adults.option_a: The escort — 200 soldiers, 70 horsemen, 200 spearmen — for one prisoner is extraordinary, revealing both the serious threat of the assassination conspiracy and the Roman military's investment in protecting a citizen. The nighttime departure (third hour of the night = 9pm) adds further secrecy. Luke may also intend irony: God's chosen apostle travels to his destiny with an imperial army as unwitting escort
adults.option_b: The escort size was normal for any prisoner
adults.option_c: Luke exaggerated the numbers
adults.option_d: Paul requested the large escort
adults.explanation: Acts 23:23 — 470 soldiers for one man: nearly half a cohort. The scale reflects both the danger (40+ assassins) and Roman seriousness about protecting citizens. The theological irony: Rome's military machine, designed to maintain pagan order, serves as God's instrument to deliver His apostle toward his destined testimony.

kids.question: Who came to accuse Paul before Governor Felix?
kids.option_a: The high priest Ananias, some elders, and a lawyer named Tertullus
kids.option_b: The Roman emperor
kids.option_c: King Herod
kids.explanation: Acts 24:1 — "And after five days Ananias the high priest descended with the elders, and with a certain orator named Tertullus, who informed the governor against Paul."

kids.question: What did Paul say he believed?
kids.option_a: Everything written in the law and the prophets, and he had hope toward God that there shall be a resurrection of the dead
kids.option_b: Only in the law of Moses
kids.option_c: Only in what the Pharisees taught
kids.explanation: Acts 24:14-15 — "So worship I the God of my fathers, believing all things which are written in the law and in the prophets: And have hope toward God... that there shall be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust."

kids.question: Why did Governor Felix keep Paul in prison for two years?
kids.option_a: He hoped Paul would give him money, and he also wanted to do the Jews a favour
kids.option_b: Paul asked to stay in prison
kids.option_c: Felix forgot about Paul
kids.explanation: Acts 24:26-27 — "He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him... But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix' room: and Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound."

teens.question: What were the charges that Tertullus brought against Paul?
teens.option_a: He called Paul a pestilent fellow, a mover of sedition among the Jews throughout the world, a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes, and one who had profaned the temple
teens.option_b: Paul had refused to pay Roman taxes
teens.option_c: Paul had stolen money from the temple treasury
teens.option_d: Paul had plotted to overthrow the governor
teens.explanation: Acts 24:5-6 — "For we have found this man a pestilent fellow, and a mover of sedition among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes: Who also hath gone about to profane the temple."

teens.question: How did Paul defend himself against the charges?
teens.option_a: He denied causing any disturbance — no one found him disputing or stirring up the people. He confessed believing in the Way, worshipping the God of his fathers, and believing all things in the law and prophets
teens.option_b: He pleaded guilty to all charges
teens.option_c: He refused to speak in his own defence
teens.option_d: He accused Tertullus of lying about everything
teens.explanation: Acts 24:12-14 — "They neither found me in the temple disputing with any man, neither raising up the people, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city... But this I confess unto thee, that after the way which they call heresy, so worship I the God of my fathers."

teens.question: What topics did Paul discuss with Felix in private?
teens.option_a: Righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come — and Felix trembled
teens.option_b: Military strategy and politics
teens.option_c: Roman law and citizenship
teens.option_d: Trade routes and commerce
teens.explanation: Acts 24:25 — "And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee."

teens.question: What was Felix hoping to get from Paul?
teens.option_a: Money — he hoped Paul or his friends would offer him a bribe for Paul's release
teens.option_b: A recommendation to the emperor
teens.option_c: A cure for a disease
teens.option_d: Permission to visit the church in Antioch
teens.explanation: Acts 24:26 — "He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him: wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him."

teens.question: How long was Paul kept in custody in Caesarea under Felix?
teens.option_a: Two years, until Felix was replaced by Porcius Festus
teens.option_b: Six months
teens.option_c: One month
teens.option_d: Five years
teens.explanation: Acts 24:27 — "But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix' room: and Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound."

adults.question: How does Tertullus's speech (24:2-8) employ standard Roman forensic rhetoric?
adults.option_a: Tertullus opens with a captatio benevolentiae (flattery of Felix, vv.2-4), then presents three charges: sedition (political — "a mover of sedition"), sectarianism (social — "ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes"), and sacrilege (religious — "profaned the temple"). The charges are strategically crafted to concern Rome (sedition), Jewish leadership (sectarianism), and the temple authorities (sacrilege)
adults.option_b: Tertullus was untrained in rhetoric
adults.option_c: He presented only one charge
adults.option_d: His speech had no rhetorical structure
adults.explanation: Acts 24:2-6 — Tertullus's speech follows classical Roman forensic form: exordium (flattery, vv.2-4), narratio (charges, vv.5-6), and peroratio (appeal, vv.8). The three charges cover political (sedition), social (sect leadership), and religious (temple profanation) dimensions — designed to appeal to Felix on every front.

adults.question: How does Paul's defence redefine "the Way" as legitimate Judaism rather than heresy?
adults.option_a: Paul insists that "the Way" is not a heretical sect but the true expression of ancestral faith: "I worship the God of my fathers, believing all things which are written in the law and in the prophets" (v.14). He claims continuity with Judaism, not departure from it. The "heresy" charge (hairesis = sect) is reframed: Christianity is Judaism fulfilled, not Judaism abandoned
adults.option_b: Paul admitted the Way was a new religion
adults.option_c: He agreed it was a heretical sect
adults.option_d: Paul made no connection to Judaism
adults.explanation: Acts 24:14-15 — Paul's defence: the Way worships Israel's God, believes Israel's Scriptures, and hopes for Israel's resurrection. He adds: "they themselves also allow, that there shall be a resurrection of the dead" (v.15) — Pharisees believe the same thing. Paul positions Christianity as within Judaism, not outside it.

adults.question: What does Felix's response — trembling at Paul's preaching on "righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come" (24:25) — reveal about the gospel's power and the human will?
adults.option_a: Felix trembled (emphobos genomenos = became terrified) because Paul's message convicted him personally — Felix was notorious for corruption and had stolen his wife Drusilla from her husband. Yet conviction did not produce conversion: "when I have a convenient season" became permanent postponement. Felix illustrates how a person can be deeply moved by truth yet refuse to submit, choosing deferral over decision
adults.option_b: Felix was physically cold
adults.option_c: Paul's preaching was ineffective
adults.option_d: Felix was already a believer
adults.explanation: Acts 24:25 — "Felix trembled, and answered, Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee." Josephus records Felix's corruption and violence (Antiquities 20.7-8). Paul's topics (righteousness, self-control, judgment) targeted Felix's known sins. The "convenient season" never came — Felix exemplifies the tragedy of deferred repentance.

adults.question: What does Felix's two-year detention of Paul reveal about Roman justice and political expediency?
adults.option_a: Felix delayed judgment not because the case was complex but because he wanted a bribe (v.26) and wanted to please the Jews (v.27). Roman justice was corrupted by personal greed and political calculation. Yet even this injustice served God's purposes: the two-year delay protected Paul from Jewish assassination plots and positioned him for transfer to a new governor who would send him to Rome
adults.option_b: Felix detained Paul for legitimate legal reasons
adults.option_c: Two years was a standard waiting period
adults.option_d: Paul was content to remain in prison
adults.explanation: Acts 24:26-27 — Dual corruption: bribery (hoping for money) and political calculation (willing to show the Jews a pleasure). Felix violated Roman legal norms by refusing to decide. Yet God's sovereignty operates even through unjust systems: the delay kept Paul safe and alive for Rome.

adults.question: How does Paul's claim of "a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men" (24:16) function in his defence?
adults.option_a: Paul claims moral integrity as evidence against the charges — a clear conscience before both God (religious dimension) and men (social dimension). This is not a claim to sinless perfection but to consistent effort to live rightly. In forensic terms, Paul offers his character as evidence: a man of good conscience does not provoke riots or profane temples
adults.option_b: Paul was claiming sinless perfection
adults.option_c: This was irrelevant to the legal case
adults.option_d: Paul was being dishonest
adults.explanation: Acts 24:16 — "And herein do I exercise myself, to have always a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men." The word "exercise" (askein) means disciplined training — Paul works at maintaining integrity. In Roman courts, character witnesses mattered. Paul presents his consistent moral effort as evidence refuting the charges.

adults.question: What does Paul's mention of "alms and offerings" (24:17) contribute to his defence?
adults.option_a: Paul reveals his purpose for coming to Jerusalem was charitable and liturgical — "to bring alms to my nation, and offerings." He was bringing the collection from Gentile churches (cf. Romans 15:25-28, 1 Corinthians 16:1-4). A man bringing charitable gifts is an unlikely temple profaner. This detail rebuts the charge of hostile intent and reveals his goodwill toward his own people
adults.option_b: Paul was trying to bribe the court
adults.option_c: The alms were for himself
adults.option_d: This detail was irrelevant
adults.explanation: Acts 24:17 — "Now after many years I came to bring alms to my nation, and offerings." This is the only Acts reference to Paul's great collection (cf. Romans 15:25-28, 2 Corinthians 8-9, Galatians 2:10). Paul's purpose was benevolent, not hostile — evidence of love for his own nation, not contempt for its institutions.

adults.question: How does Felix having "more perfect knowledge of that way" (24:22) affect his legal and moral responsibility?
adults.option_a: Felix already knew about Christianity ("having more perfect knowledge of that way") — possibly through his Jewish wife Drusilla (v.24). His knowledge made his deferral more culpable: he was not ignorant but informed, not confused but corrupt. Like Herod with John the Baptist (Mark 6:20 — "heard him gladly"), Felix was fascinated by truth but refused to submit to it
adults.option_b: Felix was completely ignorant of Christianity
adults.option_c: His knowledge excused his decisions
adults.option_d: Felix was neutral and fair
adults.explanation: Acts 24:22,24 — Felix had prior knowledge of "the Way" and sent for Paul frequently to discuss "the faith in Christ" with Drusilla. His knowledge increased his guilt: he delayed not from confusion but from corruption. Jesus taught that greater knowledge brings greater accountability (Luke 12:47-48).

adults.question: How do chapters 22-24 collectively demonstrate that Paul committed no crime against Roman or Jewish law?
adults.option_a: Through three hearings — the crowd (ch.22), the Sanhedrin (ch.23), and Felix (ch.24) — no formal charge sticks. Lysias found no Roman crime (23:29), the Sanhedrin could not agree (23:10), and Felix deferred without conviction (24:22-27). Luke systematically shows Paul declared innocent at every level, building a cumulative legal apologetic: Christianity threatens neither Rome nor Judaism
adults.option_b: Paul was found guilty at one hearing
adults.option_c: Only one hearing took place
adults.option_d: The legal proceedings were irrelevant to Luke
adults.explanation: Acts 22-24 — Lysias: "nothing worthy of death or of bonds" (23:29). The Sanhedrin: split with Pharisees defending Paul (23:9). Felix: no verdict, only delay (24:22-27). Luke's legal apologetic accumulates: three proceedings, zero convictions. Christianity is legally vindicated at every turn.

kids.question: What did Paul say when Governor Festus asked if he wanted to go to Jerusalem for trial?
kids.option_a: I appeal unto Caesar — meaning he asked to have his trial before the Roman emperor
kids.option_b: I want to go to Jerusalem
kids.option_c: I want to stay here in Caesarea
kids.option_d: I want to go home to Tarsus
kids.explanation: Acts 25:10-11 — "I stand at Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be judged... I appeal unto Caesar."

kids.question: What king came to visit Governor Festus in Caesarea?
kids.option_a: King Agrippa and his sister Bernice
kids.option_b: King Herod the Great
kids.option_c: The king of Egypt
kids.explanation: Acts 25:13 — "And after certain days king Agrippa and Bernice came unto Caesarea to salute Festus."

kids.question: What did Festus tell King Agrippa about Paul's case?
kids.option_a: The Jews had questions about their own religion and about one Jesus, who was dead, whom Paul said to be alive
kids.option_b: Paul had robbed the temple
kids.option_c: Paul was a dangerous criminal
kids.explanation: Acts 25:19 — "But had certain questions against him of their own superstition, and of one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive."

teens.question: Why did Paul appeal to Caesar instead of being tried in Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Paul knew the Jewish leaders wanted to ambush and kill him on the way to Jerusalem, and as a Roman citizen he had the right to be tried by the emperor himself
teens.option_b: Paul wanted to see Rome as a tourist
teens.option_c: Paul was afraid of Festus
teens.option_d: Paul wanted to impress the other prisoners
teens.explanation: Acts 25:3,9-11 — "And the Jews... laying wait in the way to kill him... Then said Paul, I stand at Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be judged... I appeal unto Caesar."

teens.question: What was Festus's problem with Paul's case?
teens.option_a: He had no clear charges to write to the emperor — the accusations were about Jewish religious disputes, not Roman crimes
teens.option_b: He thought Paul was guilty but could not prove it
teens.option_c: He was afraid of Paul's power
teens.option_d: He did not understand Latin well enough
teens.explanation: Acts 25:26-27 — "Of whom I have no certain thing to write unto my lord. Wherefore I have brought him forth before you... that, after examination had, I might have somewhat to write. For it seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes laid against him."

teens.question: Why did Festus want King Agrippa to hear Paul's case?
teens.option_a: Because Agrippa was an expert in Jewish customs and religion, and Festus needed help understanding the charges so he could write a report to Caesar
teens.option_b: Because Agrippa was Paul's relative
teens.option_c: Because Agrippa was the emperor's representative
teens.option_d: Because Agrippa was a judge in all criminal cases
teens.explanation: Acts 25:26 — "Of whom I have no certain thing to write unto my lord... I might have somewhat to write." Acts 26:3 confirms: "I know thee to be expert in all customs and questions which are among the Jews."

teens.question: What did Festus say to King Agrippa about the accusations against Paul?
teens.option_a: The accusers brought no charge of such things as he had expected — they had questions about their own religion and about one Jesus, who was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive
teens.option_b: Paul was charged with murder and theft
teens.option_c: The Romans accused Paul of treason
teens.option_d: Paul confessed to all the charges
teens.explanation: Acts 25:18-19 — "Against whom when the accusers stood up, they brought none accusation of such things as I supposed: But had certain questions against him of their own superstition, and of one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive."

teens.question: How did King Agrippa respond when Festus told him about Paul?
teens.option_a: He said he would like to hear the man himself — and Festus arranged for Paul to appear before him the next day
teens.option_b: He said he was not interested in the case
teens.option_c: He told Festus to release Paul immediately
teens.option_d: He refused to get involved in Jewish matters
teens.explanation: Acts 25:22 — "Then Agrippa said unto Festus, I would also hear the man myself. To morrow, said he, thou shalt hear him."

adults.question: What does Paul's appeal to Caesar (provocatio ad Caesarem) signify legally and theologically?
adults.option_a: Legally, as a Roman citizen, Paul exercised his right to have his case heard by the emperor — removing it from provincial jurisdiction. Theologically, the appeal is the human mechanism through which God's promise ("thou must bear witness at Rome," 23:11) is fulfilled. Paul's legal right becomes the vehicle of divine destiny
adults.option_b: The appeal was illegal for Roman citizens
adults.option_c: Paul acted impulsively without divine guidance
adults.option_d: The appeal delayed the gospel
adults.explanation: Acts 25:11-12 — "I appeal unto Caesar. Then Festus... answered, Hast thou appealed unto Caesar? unto Caesar shalt thou go." The Lex Julia granted citizens the right of appeal. God uses Roman jurisprudence as the means to fulfil His promise: legal rights become the pathway to prophetic destiny.

adults.question: What does Festus's summary of the case ("one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive," 25:19) reveal about how outsiders perceived the Christian message?
adults.option_a: Festus distils the entire case to one question: is a dead man alive? This outsider's summary is unintentionally accurate — the resurrection of Jesus is indeed the central issue. Festus sees it as a puzzling Jewish superstition; Luke presents it as the crux of history. The governor's bemused incomprehension highlights the scandal of resurrection to the Gentile mind
adults.option_b: Festus fully understood Christian theology
adults.option_c: The resurrection was not central to the dispute
adults.option_d: Festus was sympathetic to Paul's message
adults.explanation: Acts 25:19 — "One Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive." This is the entire gospel from a Roman politician's perspective: a dead man claimed to be alive. Festus reduces the case to its essence without realising he has stated the most important claim in human history. The simplicity is devastating.

adults.question: How does Festus's dilemma (needing charges to send to Caesar) serve Luke's apologetic purpose?
adults.option_a: Festus cannot formulate charges because there are no charges — "I have no certain thing to write" (v.26). The Roman governor's inability to identify a crime is powerful vindication: two governors (Felix and Festus) and the Sanhedrin have been unable to convict Paul of anything. Luke demonstrates that Christianity is not criminal; it is a religious dispute that Roman law should not prosecute
adults.option_b: Festus was simply incompetent
adults.option_c: He had clear charges but chose not to write them
adults.option_d: Luke invented the dilemma
adults.explanation: Acts 25:25-27 — "I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death... Of whom I have no certain thing to write... it seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes." The governor's legal embarrassment is Luke's theological vindication: no crime exists because Christianity is not criminal.

adults.question: What is the dramatic significance of Paul's hearing before Agrippa being held "with great pomp" (25:23)?
adults.option_a: The hearing is staged in the audience chamber with military commanders and prominent men — maximum secular splendour. Yet the true power dynamic is inverted: the prisoner Paul possesses the truth that kings and governors need. Luke contrasts worldly "pomp" (phantasia = display/show) with the reality of the gospel. The one in chains is freer than the ones on thrones
adults.option_b: The pomp honoured Paul
adults.option_c: Agrippa was being humble
adults.option_d: The setting was unimportant to Luke
adults.explanation: Acts 25:23 — "Agrippa was come, and Bernice, with great pomp (phantasia)." The Greek word "phantasia" means show/display — the same root as "phantom." The contrast is deliberate: outward show vs. inward reality. The chained apostle has what the crowned king lacks — truth, hope, and freedom.

adults.question: How does Acts 25 fulfil Jesus' prediction in Luke 21:12-13 and Acts 9:15?
adults.option_a: Jesus predicted: "They shall bring you before kings and rulers for my name's sake. And it shall turn to you for a testimony" (Luke 21:12-13). Acts 9:15: Paul would "bear my name before the Gentiles, and kings." Now Paul stands before a king (Agrippa), governors (Felix, Festus), and will eventually reach the emperor. The trials are not obstacles but platforms — persecution becomes proclamation
adults.option_b: These predictions were not fulfilled
adults.option_c: Only the Agrippa hearing counts
adults.option_d: Paul never appeared before a king
adults.explanation: Acts 25 — Paul before Festus (governor) and Agrippa (king) = Luke 21:12-13 and Acts 9:15 fulfilled. The trial is transformed from prosecution into pulpit. Every legal proceeding in chapters 22-26 is simultaneously a gospel presentation. Opposition creates opportunities for witness before the powerful.

adults.question: What does Festus's behaviour — wanting to "do the Jews a pleasure" by sending Paul to Jerusalem (25:9) — reveal about political corruption and its limits?
adults.option_a: Like Felix before him (24:27), Festus prioritises political expediency over justice. Yet Paul's appeal to Caesar removes the case from corrupt provincial politics. God uses legal rights to protect against political manipulation. Luke shows that while Roman justice is imperfect (governors are corruptible), the system itself provides safeguards (the right of appeal) that serve God's purposes
adults.option_b: Festus was acting justly
adults.option_c: Paul had no legal options
adults.option_d: Roman justice was entirely corrupt
adults.explanation: Acts 25:9 — "Festus, willing to do the Jews a pleasure, answered Paul..." Festus repeats Felix's error (24:27 — "willing to shew the Jews a pleasure"). Both governors sacrifice justice for political favour. Yet Paul's appeal to Caesar (a legal right) overrides gubernatorial corruption. God works through imperfect systems.

adults.question: How does the Agrippa-Festus dynamic function in the trial narrative?
adults.option_a: Festus provides Roman administrative perspective (political charges) while Agrippa provides Jewish religious expertise (theological charges). Together they cover all possible angles of accusation. Yet both find Paul innocent — the Jewish expert and the Roman administrator agree. Luke uses their complementary competencies to provide comprehensive vindication
adults.option_b: Only Festus was involved in the case
adults.option_c: Agrippa was hostile to Paul
adults.option_d: Their perspectives were identical
adults.explanation: Festus: "no crime worthy of death" (25:25). Agrippa: "this man might have been set at liberty" (26:32). The Roman administrator and the Jewish king independently reach the same conclusion: Paul is innocent. Luke builds his case through expert witnesses from both jurisdictions.

adults.question: What is the irony of Festus finding it "unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes" (25:27)?
adults.option_a: The supreme irony: a Roman governor must send a prisoner to the emperor but cannot explain what crime he committed. The prisoner has been investigated by multiple authorities over multiple years and no charge stands. Paul is a prisoner without a crime — echoing Jesus, who was convicted without guilt (Luke 23:4,14,22). The legal system has become absurd because the "crime" is the gospel
adults.option_b: There was nothing ironic about the situation
adults.option_c: Festus easily identified the crimes
adults.option_d: Paul had committed obvious offences
adults.explanation: Acts 25:27 — "It seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes." The governor admits the absurdity: Paul is a prisoner, yet no crime exists. The entire Roman legal apparatus — governors, soldiers, hearings — has been activated for a man whose only offence is proclaiming that a dead man is alive.

kids.question: What question did Paul ask King Agrippa about the prophets?
kids.option_a: King Agrippa, believest thou the prophets? I know that thou believest
kids.option_b: Do you want to hear a story?
kids.option_c: Have you ever been to Jerusalem?
kids.explanation: Acts 26:27 — "King Agrippa, believest thou the prophets? I know that thou believest."

kids.question: What did Paul say Jesus told him to do on the road to Damascus?
kids.option_a: To be a minister and a witness, and to open people's eyes so they can turn from darkness to light
kids.option_b: To go and build a new temple
kids.option_c: To become a farmer in Galilee
kids.explanation: Acts 26:16-18 — "I have appeared unto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witness... To open their eyes, and to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God."

kids.question: What did King Agrippa say to Paul after hearing his story?
kids.option_a: Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian
kids.option_b: I want to be baptised right now
kids.option_c: I do not believe anything you said
kids.option_d: You must be sent to prison
kids.explanation: Acts 26:28 — "Then Agrippa said unto Paul, Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian."

teens.question: How did Paul describe his former life as a Pharisee?
teens.option_a: He lived after the strictest sect of the Jewish religion — he was a Pharisee who persecuted the church, imprisoning and punishing believers, and even voted for their death
teens.option_b: He was a quiet scholar who avoided conflict
teens.option_c: He was a merchant who traded goods
teens.option_d: He was a farmer in Galilee
teens.explanation: Acts 26:5,9-11 — "After the most straitest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee... I verily thought with myself, that I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth... I shut up in prison... I punished them oft... and when they were put to death, I gave my voice against them."

teens.question: What was Festus's reaction when Paul spoke about the resurrection?
teens.option_a: Festus said with a loud voice "Paul, thou art beside thyself; much learning doth make thee mad"
teens.option_b: Festus was deeply moved and started crying
teens.option_c: Festus fell asleep during Paul's speech
teens.option_d: Festus agreed with everything Paul said
teens.explanation: Acts 26:24 — "And as he thus spake for himself, Festus said with a loud voice, Paul, thou art beside thyself; much learning doth make thee mad."

teens.question: How did Paul respond to Festus's accusation of madness?
teens.option_a: I am not mad, most noble Festus; but speak forth the words of truth and soberness — these things were not done in a corner
teens.option_b: He apologised for being too passionate
teens.option_c: He stopped speaking entirely
teens.option_d: He agreed that it sounded crazy
teens.explanation: Acts 26:25-26 — "But he said, I am not mad, most noble Festus; but speak forth the words of truth and soberness. For the king knoweth of these things, before whom also I speak freely: for I am persuaded that none of these things are hidden from him; for this thing was not done in a corner."

teens.question: What did Agrippa and Festus agree about Paul after the hearing?
teens.option_a: This man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds — he might have been set at liberty if he had not appealed unto Caesar
teens.option_b: Paul was guilty of treason
teens.option_c: Paul should be punished severely
teens.option_d: Paul needed to stay in prison longer
teens.explanation: Acts 26:31-32 — "And when they were gone aside, they talked between themselves, saying, This man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds. Then said Agrippa unto Festus, This man might have been set at liberty, if he had not appealed unto Caesar."

teens.question: What did Paul say he wished for everyone who heard him that day?
teens.option_a: That they might become such as he was, except for his chains — meaning he wished they would all become Christians
teens.option_b: That they would become rulers of Rome
teens.option_c: That they would leave Caesarea and travel the world
teens.option_d: That they would forget everything he said
teens.explanation: Acts 26:29 — "I would to God, that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were both almost, and altogether such as I am, except these bonds."

adults.question: How does Paul's third telling of his conversion (Acts 26) differ from the first two, and what does it add?
adults.option_a: This version adds the most detailed commission from Jesus: "to open their eyes, to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God" (v.18). It also adds "it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks" (v.14) and omits Ananias's role entirely — Paul receives his commission directly from Christ. Each retelling emphasises different aspects for the specific audience
adults.option_b: All three accounts are identical
adults.option_c: This version contradicts the others
adults.option_d: The differences indicate fabrication
adults.explanation: Acts 26:14-18 — Unique to this account: "kick against the pricks" (v.14, a Greek proverb), the comprehensive commission (v.16-18), the light-darkness/Satan-God framework (v.18). Ananias is omitted because Paul argues before royalty that his authority comes directly from Christ. Each retelling is tailored, not contradictory.

adults.question: What does "it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks" (26:14) mean?
adults.option_a: The phrase is a Greek proverb about an ox kicking against the sharp goad that drives it — futile resistance against a superior force. Applied to Paul, it means his persecution of the church was futile resistance against God's sovereign purposes. Paul was fighting against God and harming only himself. The proverb, from Greek literature (Euripides, Aeschylus), shows Jesus speaking in terms Paul's educated mind would understand
adults.option_b: Paul was literally kicking something
adults.option_c: The phrase is a Hebrew expression
adults.option_d: It means Paul was physically injured
adults.explanation: Acts 26:14 — "It is hard for thee to kick against the pricks (kentra)." The goad metaphor (from Euripides' Bacchae and Aeschylus' Agamemnon) describes futile resistance against divine power. Jesus uses a Greek idiom for Saul the Hellenised Jew. Paul's persecution was self-destructive opposition to God's irresistible purpose.

adults.question: How does Paul's commission in 26:16-18 summarise the entire mission theology of Acts?
adults.option_a: The commission contains Acts' complete mission theology: Paul as "minister and witness" (v.16), sent to open eyes (v.18a), turn from darkness to light (v.18b), from Satan's power to God (v.18c), offer forgiveness of sins (v.18d), and inheritance among the sanctified by faith (v.18e). Every element — witness, illumination, liberation, forgiveness, inheritance, faith — encapsulates the gospel's content and method
adults.option_b: The commission was only about preaching
adults.option_c: It applied only to Paul personally
adults.option_d: Mission theology is not found in Acts
adults.explanation: Acts 26:16-18 — The commission layers five salvific effects: (1) eyes opened (illumination), (2) darkness → light (epistemological transformation), (3) Satan → God (spiritual liberation), (4) forgiveness of sins (justification), (5) inheritance among the sanctified by faith (adoption/eschatological hope). This is the gospel in miniature.

adults.question: What does Agrippa's response ("Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian," 26:28) reveal?
adults.option_a: Whether sincere or sarcastic (the Greek en oligo is ambiguous — "in a little" = briefly/almost/easily), Agrippa's response shows the gospel pressing for a decision. Paul's response treats it as genuine engagement: "I would to God... all were such as I am." Even a possible deflection becomes a gospel opportunity. Agrippa acknowledges the force of Paul's argument without submitting to it — the tragedy of the unconverted intellectual
adults.option_b: Agrippa became a Christian
adults.option_c: The response was purely sarcastic
adults.option_d: Paul was disappointed by Agrippa's answer
adults.explanation: Acts 26:28-29 — "En oligo" is debated: "in a short time" (you'd make me a Christian quickly) or "with little effort" (you almost persuade me). Either way, Paul seizes it: "I wish all who hear me today were such as I am." Whether Agrippa deflects or nearly yields, Paul turns the moment toward the gospel.

adults.question: What does the verdict "This man might have been set at liberty, if he had not appealed unto Caesar" (26:32) accomplish in Luke's narrative?
adults.option_a: It is the climactic vindication: the highest Jewish authority (Agrippa) and the highest Roman authority in Judaea (Festus) agree Paul is innocent. The appeal to Caesar, which seems to prevent release, actually fulfils divine purpose — Paul must reach Rome (23:11). Human innocence and divine necessity intersect: Paul is legally innocent yet divinely bound for Rome
adults.option_b: The verdict meant Paul was guilty
adults.option_c: The appeal to Caesar was a mistake
adults.option_d: Agrippa's opinion did not matter
adults.explanation: Acts 26:31-32 — "This man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds." The progression: Lysias (23:29), Felix (24:25 — no verdict but no condemnation), Festus (25:25), Agrippa (26:31-32). Four officials, zero convictions. The appeal to Caesar is not a legal error but a divine vehicle. Innocence and destiny converge.

adults.question: How does Paul's statement "this thing was not done in a corner" (26:26) function as an apologetic principle?
adults.option_a: Paul claims the events of the gospel — Jesus' ministry, death, and resurrection — are public, historical, and verifiable, not private myths or secret mysteries. This is an appeal to the historical nature of Christianity: it is based on events that happened in real places, among real people, in full view of history. Faith is not blind but based on public evidence
adults.option_b: Paul was speaking about his trial only
adults.option_c: The gospel was a private matter
adults.option_d: Paul was referring to his years in hiding
adults.explanation: Acts 26:26 — "This thing was not done in a corner." Paul appeals to the public, historical nature of the gospel events. The crucifixion was public (under Pontius Pilate), the resurrection had hundreds of witnesses (1 Corinthians 15:6), and Paul's own conversion was known throughout the empire. Christianity stakes its claim on history, not mythology.

adults.question: What does Paul's wish — "except these bonds" (26:29) — reveal about his understanding of suffering and freedom?
adults.option_a: Paul offers everything he has to Agrippa — his faith, his joy, his calling — but not his chains. This is both pastoral (he would not wish imprisonment on anyone) and deeply theological: Paul's chains are the cost of discipleship, not the content of the gospel. He is genuinely free despite chains. True freedom is internal (relationship with Christ), not external (absence of chains)
adults.option_b: Paul was complaining about his imprisonment
adults.option_c: He wanted Agrippa to also be imprisoned
adults.option_d: The chains were merely metaphorical
adults.explanation: Acts 26:29 — "Both almost, and altogether such as I am, except these bonds." Paul generously offers everything positive and withholds only suffering. Yet the irony is profound: the man in chains is freer than the king on the throne. Paul's bonds are the evidence of his mission, not its failure.

adults.question: How does Acts 26 represent the climax of Paul's defence speeches and their collective theological contribution?
adults.option_a: Acts 26 is the fullest and most theologically developed of Paul's defence speeches, integrating his biography, Christology, soteriology, and missiology. The progression from ch.22 (Jewish crowd) through ch.24 (Felix) to ch.26 (Agrippa) shows increasing theological depth and wider audience. Together, the speeches demonstrate that the gospel is historically grounded, scripturally rooted, personally transformative, and universally relevant
adults.option_b: Chapter 26 is the weakest of Paul's speeches
adults.option_c: The speeches are repetitive without development
adults.option_d: Paul never discussed theology in his defences
adults.explanation: Acts 26 is the culmination: fuller commission (vv.16-18), stronger prophetic argument (vv.22-23), direct evangelistic appeal (vv.27-29), and comprehensive verdict (vv.31-32).
